The docs show me that I can make batch api calls. I have tried that, but I must be missing something.

```python
get_creatives_using_batch_api()  # it gives me empty objects
```

```python
    api_batch.execute()
    print('--------- Result with batch ------------')

get_creatives_one_by_one()  # it works!
get_creatives_using_batch_api()  # it only gives me ids
```

issue_title: Automatic file download triggered on workflow state change
issue_body:
When a workflow is assigned to the file content type, any workflow state change triggers the file to be downloaded.  This is because the fti url_expr for the view action returns just the object url:

`<action
      action_id="view"
      category="object"
      condition_expr=""
      i18n:attributes="title"
      title="View"
      url_expr="string:${object_url}"
      visible="True">
    <permission value="View"/>
  </action>`

Products.CMFFormController.Actions.RedirectToAction is getting the view expression from the fti and returning the direct url to the file which triggers the download.

The view action url_expr should be:

`url_expr="string:${object_url}/view"`

issue_title: Consistent url to the latest release.

It would be helpful if you offered a consistent url to latest compatible packages release.
Something along the lines of https://github.com/mozilla/sops/releases/download/2/sops_2_latest_amd64.deb would always point to the latest release of sops2.

issue_title: "put" not available for dns_records
issue_body:
Hi,

the readme states that put is available for `dns_records`-operations:

GET | PUT | POST | PATCH | DELETE | API call
-- | -- | -- | -- | -- | --
(...) | (...) | (...) | (...) | (...) | (...)
`GET` | `PUT` | `POST` |   | `DELETE` | /zones/:identifier/dns_records
(...) | (...) | (...) | (...) | (...) | (...)

However when using `dns_records.put()` (`cf.zones.dns_records.put(zone_id, data=record)`) the SDK throws an error: `CloudFlare.exceptions.CloudFlareAPIError: Method PUT not available for that URI.`

At least the readme should be correct about the available methods, but ideally an idempotent `PUT` should be available for `dns_records` to avoid duplicate records.

issue_title: Move soma packger logic in the debian stuff
issue_body:
We are using some hacks to build the deb packages, like moving the `openquake.cfg` and other stuff into `openquake/engine/`. issue_title: PGPKey.pubkey.subkeys don't have parent attribute.
issue_body:
I encountered a strange bug:
```
======================================================================
ERROR: MockAndMonkeyLayer ConfigLayer PGPConfigLayer test_decrypt (mailman_pgp.runners.tests.test_incoming.TestIncoming)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/johny/gsoc/psf/mailman/mailman-pgp/src/mailman_pgp/runners/tests/test_incoming.py", line 112, in test_decrypt
    emsg = self.pgp_list.pubkey.encrypt(pmsg)
  File "/home/johny/gsoc/psf/mailman/PGPy/pgpy/decorators.py", line 125, in _action
    with self.usage(key, kwargs.get('user', None)) as _key:
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/contextlib.py", line 82, in __enter__
    return next(self.gen)
  File "/home/johny/gsoc/psf/mailman/PGPy/pgpy/decorators.py", line 92, in usage
    if self.flags & set(_key._get_key_flags(user)):
  File "/home/johny/gsoc/psf/mailman/PGPy/pgpy/pgp.py", line 1708, in _get_key_flags
    return next(self.self_signatures).key_flags
  File "/home/johny/gsoc/psf/mailman/PGPy/pgpy/pgp.py", line 1359, in self_signatures
    else (self.parent.fingerprint.keyid, SignatureType.Subkey_Binding)
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'fingerprint'
----------------------------------------------------------------------
```
With keys generated like this:  https://gitlab.com/J08nY/mailman-pgp/blob/master/src/mailman_pgp/pgp/keygen.py

This all comes down to the pubkeys subkeys not having a proper `parent` attribute. While the original private keys subkeys have the `parent` attribute pointing at the private key.

I think the problem is around [pgp.py#L1320](https://github.com/SecurityInnovation/PGPy/blob/master/pgpy/pgp.py#L1320) where the pubkeys subkeys are created and it's obvious they won't have any `parent` set because they have no way to know about the newly created parent.

```python
                # get the public half of each subkey
                for skid, subkey in self.subkeys.items():
                    pub.subkeys[skid] = subkey.pubkey
```

I think doing something like
```python
                # get the public half of each subkey
                for skid, subkey in self.subkeys.items():
                    pub.subkeys[skid] = subkey.pubkey
                    pub.subkeys[skid].parent = pub
```
where `pub` is the newly created public sibling.

issue_title: ValidationError returned by CloudFormation.Client.update_stack when updating template description
issue_body:
CloudFormation.Client.update_stack returns an error if there are no changes to be applied to the stack:
```
botocore.exceptions.ClientError: An error occurred (ValidationError) when calling the UpdateStack operation: No updates are to be performed. 
```

This means you have to defensively check whether there are going to be updates before calling it by checking for equality with the current template and parameters.

However, it also returns this error in some cases where there _are_ updates which are accepted by the CFN APIs/UI directly. For example, changing the Description property of the template, which means an equality check fails, but an update still fails when it shouldn't.

I've created a basic script to show the issue - updating the template via the Cloudformation UI succeeds and the description is changed:
```python
import boto3,  logging

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)

cfnclient = boto3.client('cloudformation')

template_body="""
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: "2010-09-09"
Description: >
  Original description
Resources:
  WaitHandle: 
    Type: "AWS::CloudFormation::WaitConditionHandle"
  """

logging.info("Create stack and wait")
cfnclient.create_stack(StackName="ChangeDescriptionTest", TemplateBody=template_body)
waiter = cfnclient.get_waiter('stack_create_complete').wait(StackName='ChangeDescriptionTest')

changed_template_body="""
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: "2010-09-09"
Description: >
  New description
Resources:
  WaitHandle: 
    Type: "AWS::CloudFormation::WaitConditionHandle"
  """

logging.info("Only perform update if there are changes")
if template_body != changed_template_body:
    #There are changes, but receive a validation error
    cfnclient.update_stack(StackName='ChangeDescriptionTest', TemplateBody=changed_template_body)
```

issue_title: Segfault on import in virtualenv
issue_body:
While trying to install pygit2 on a virtualenv, I ran into several segfault issues. This is using python 3.5.1, which is not officially supported, but my distro (Arch) manages to provide a stock package for it so I decided to give it a shot.

I started with `libgit2` globally installed on the system and followed the install guide:

```
$ virtualenv foo/
$ cd foo/
$ source bin/activate
$ pip install pygit2
$ python -c 'import pygit2'
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
```

What I tried:
  * Maybe `libgit2` and `pygit2` versions don't match: They do
  * Maybe python can't locate the `libgit.so` file (even though I don't get the "Can't locate file" error): Tried to locally build `libgit2` as the install docs suggest, no luck here 
  * Fiddled with `LIBGIT2` and `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` env vars: still no luck

I then tried to use the distro package for `pygit2`:

```
# pacman -S python-pygit2
$ python -c 'import pygit2; print("Success!")'
Success
```

So it works. As far as I can tell looking at the package definition files, Arch maintainers don't seem to apply any custom patches, so it looks like vanilla `pygit2` to me. I then tried using the distro package in my virtualenv:

```
$ virtualenv --system-site-packages foo/
$ cd foo/
$ source bin/activate
$ python -c 'import pygit2'
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
```

So it looks like it is somehow due to interaction with the virtualenv. While digging, I discovered python's `venv` module, which seems to offer more or less the same functionalities as `virtualenv`, so I gave it a shot. This time, both scenarios below worked (command is available as `pyvenv` on my system, but I reckon you can also use `python -m venv` to the same effect):

```
## Using local venv pygit2 install and global libgit2
$ pyvenv foo/
$ cd foo
$ source bin/activate
$ pip install pygit2
$ pthon -c 'import pygit2; print("Success!")'
Success!
```

```
## Using global pygit2 and global libgit2
$ pyvenv --system-site-packages foo/
$ cd foo
$ source bin/activate
$ pthon -c 'import pygit2; print("Success!")'
Success!
```

This might not even be directly related to pygit, so feel free to close this issue. At least there's some kind of ref to the issue if anyone runs into the same problem

Cheers!

issue_title: URLs passed to repo.git.remote in reverse in Remote.set_url
issue_body:
In [Remote.set_url](https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/master/git/remote.py#L471), the new_url and old_url seem to be passed to the repo.git.remote command in the wrong order if old_url is provided:

```
def set_url(self, new_url, old_url=None, **kwargs):
        """Configure URLs on current remote (cf command git remote set_url)
        This command manages URLs on the remote.
        :param new_url: string being the URL to add as an extra remote URL
        :param old_url: when set, replaces this URL with new_url for the remote
        :return: self
        """
        scmd = 'set-url'
        kwargs['insert_kwargs_after'] = scmd
        if old_url:
            self.repo.git.remote(scmd, self.name, old_url, new_url, **kwargs)
        else:
            self.repo.git.remote(scmd, self.name, new_url, **kwargs)
        return self
```

The [git scm](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-remote) says they should be the other way around:

`git remote set-url [--push] <name> <newurl> [<oldurl>]`

This leads to the following behaviour:

```
remote = repo.create_remote("upstream", "place")
remote.set_url("new_place", "place")
```

results in:

```
git.exc.GitCommandError: Cmd('git') failed due to: exit code(128)
  cmdline: git remote set-url upstream place new_place
  stderr: 'fatal: No such URL found: new_place'
```

issue_title: Sorting in TreeModifier.set_done() seems to be inconsistent with git's own sorting -> breaks repository
issue_body:
git enforces tree objects to be sorted by name. GitPython enables users to sort their tree's by calling TreeModifier.set_done(), see:
https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/blob/master/git/objects/tree.py#L45

This sorting somehow seems to be inconsistent with git's own sorting mechanism, which means adding a tree might break the git repository. This means "git fsck" will throw errors and some remotes (gitlab for example) will no accept the commit. Anyways this bug leaves the git object store in an broken state which cannot be fixed easily, especially if there is much history after the broken commit/tree. I had to delete multiple branches from projects I manage because of their broken state so far.

About the problem itself:

GitPython uses core Python functions to sort the file list. This looks correct, but has one major difference to what git itself does. If you have two files beginning with the same chars (for example "file" and "file.second") their ordering will differ:

What git does:
1) "file.second"
2) "file"

What GitPython does:
1) "file"
2) "file.second"

We found this problem using fabdeploit for deployments using git, which allows us to add/removed files to/from the git repository. This way tree's will be modified which means sorting is essential.

Please fix this. ;-)

```
        return
```

After this an #13 collection of types works in Python 3.5 on Ubuntu 16.04.

issue_title: Fix documentation bug for custom locator
Get Table Cell fails to give correct element if there are nested tables
issue_body:
If you have a table that has rows that contain nested tables within them, and these nested tables have table header elements, it turns out that these "th" elements from the nested table are treated as the first elements in the outer table row.

The offending line is in _tableelement.py at about line 38 and looks like:

columns = rows[row_index].find_elements_by_tag_name('th')

The problem seems to be that the find_elements_by_tag_name being used here will dig deep into anything found in the indexed for (nested or otherwise) and put any "th" elements at the start of the constructed array of column values. It shouldn't do this. It should only pick up "th" rows if they are elements of the row in question.

issue_title: Call to django-admin assumes django-admin.py in same dir as sys.executable
issue_body:
# Problem
The installer (main.py) calls `django.create_project(config_data)` line 32, which fails with error:
```
subprocess.CalledProcessError: Command '/usr/bin/python /usr/bin/django-admin.py startproject <project name> .' returned non-zero exit status 2
```
if `django-admin.py` is not in the same directory as the python executable.
In my case here, the system executable is in `/usr/bin/python` but the django-admin.py is in `/usr/local/bin/django-admin.py`

# Set up
My `PATH` is `/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin`

May be of interest, I am using a Docker [(What is Docker?)](https://www.docker.com/whatisdocker) container with a clean Debain/Jessie, `apt-get install build-essential git python python-dev python-setuptools software-properties-common sqlite3 libtiff5-dev libjpeg62-turbo-dev zlib1g-dev libfreetype6-dev`
then `easy_install pip `
then `pip install djangocms_installer`
and finally `djangocms -p . <project name>`


# Workaround

Make a symlink: `ln -s /usr/local/bin/django-admin.py /usr/bin/django-admin.py`


# Notes
This could very well be an error on my part in set up/configuration, but I can't think of any point at which I could have easily altered config settings/paths. As I am using a super clean environment and the tools I'm using are aimed at out-of-the-box functionality, perhaps the djangocms_installer source shouldn't make the assumption above?

If interested, here is my Dockerfile:
```
FROM debian:jessie
# ORIGINAL MAINTAINER Matt Bentley <mbentley@mbentley.net>
# https://github.com/mbentley/docker-django-uwsgi-nginx
MAINTAINER Josh Atkins <josh.atkins.dev@gmail.com>

RUN (apt-get update && DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get install -y build-essential git python python-dev python-setuptools software-properties-common sqlite3 &&\
			apt-get install -y libtiff5-dev libjpeg62-turbo-dev zlib1g-dev libfreetype6-dev)

RUN (easy_install pip)
RUN pip install djangocms_installer && ln -s /usr/local/bin/django-admin.py /usr/bin/django-admin.py

# alongside the Dockerfile, need "./project_dir/requirements.txt"
ADD project_dir/requirements.txt /opt/django/project_dir/requirements.txt
RUN pip install -r /opt/django/project_dir/requirements.txt
ADD . /opt/django/

VOLUME ["/opt/django/project_dir"]
EXPOSE 8000
```

issue_title: Deploy issue connected to another network
issue_body:
I believe I found a bug when connected to non-FRC networking equipment. 

In \pyfrc\robotpy\installer.py, I'm having unpacking issues.

socket.getaddrinfo('roborio-4901-frc.local', None) returns
[(<AddressFamily.AF_INET6: 23>,  0,  0,  '',  ('fe80::280:2fff:fe17:3af1%8', 0, 0, 8)), (<AddressFamily.AF_INET: 2>, 0, 0, '', ('129.252.23.54', 0))]

However, socket.getaddrinfo('129.252.23.54', None) returns
[(<AddressFamily.AF_INET: 2>, 0, 0, '', ('129.252.23.54', 0))]

The first item in the list is invalid for the unpacking done by the 'for' loop on line 333.

Specifically the issue has to do with the last tuple returned. From the Python socket documentation: 
"sockaddr is a tuple describing a socket address, whose format depends on the returned family (a (address, port) **2-tuple for AF_INET**, a (address, port, flow info, scope id) **4-tuple for AF_INET6)**, and is meant to be passed to the socket.connect() method."

Here is my fix:
# pick the first address that is sock_stream
        for addr in addrs:
            if len(addr[4]) == 2: # Only addr with IPv4 addresses
                (_, t, _, _, (ip, _)) = addr
                if t == socket.SOCK_STREAM:
                    print("-> Found %s at %s" % (hostname, ip))
                    print()
                    hostname = ip
                    break

issue_title: Pipenv duplicates 2nd-level dependencies and "file" dependencies
issue_body:
```toml
# Pipfile
[[source]]
url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"

[packages]
dj-paypal = "*"
"1793fa7" = {file = "https://github.com/jleclanche/dj-stripe/archive/master.tar.gz"}
```

This pipfile depends on two packages:
* dj-stripe, pulled from git master tarball (`https://github.com/jleclanche/dj-stripe/archive/master.tar.gz`)
* dj-paypal, which itself depends on dj-stripe

The resulting environment is corrupt however: dj-stripe will be installed twice, in mismatching ways.

pipenv should either resolve the file dependency first and understand that it is dj-stripe and satisfies the dj-paypal "dj-stripe" dependency, or at least warn/error rather than create a corrupt env.

issue_title: Extraneous Content in Pipfile.lock
issue_body:
It seems the opinion of the community is pretty unanimous:

### NOBODY WANTS LOCAL DEV OS JUNK IN `Pipfile.lock`

See #753

I don't know too many people that use Mac OS in production, so many people using Heroku or similar tools will have to deal with their app being in a perpetual [fugue state](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fugue_state).

I haven't yelled on GitHub before... but I also haven't come across a thread on an open source project where **everyone** wanted to remove a pretty bad feature from something and then the core maintainer popped in and said:

> going to remain, for now.

Then locked the thread to avoid hearing any other opinions on the matter (outside of the DPRK, we have an "Unsubscribe" button on the right side of the thread when we don't want the chatter).

issue_title: consider: please change the names of these files
issue_body:
Rather than naming it "Pipfile.lock", could we call these something else before they catch on?

1. File extensions are a good idea; [Filefiles](https://github.com/cobyism/Filefile) are an antipattern.  This new file should be `<something>.pipfile`.  Lots of software (the mac's Finder, windows's Explorer, Nautilus, Vim, Emacs, Apache, Nginx, anything that knows how to read `/etc/mime.types`) can infer a MIME type from an extension easily and only from a full filename with great difficulty if at all.  Even if it's always called `Pipfile.pipfile`, please consider a unique file extension.
2. A file whose name ends in `.lock` is a file that you call `flock()` on, or perhaps, over NFS, that you call `open(..., O_CREAT | O_EXCL)` on.  In other words, it is a _lock_.  I realize that this naming convention is sort of by extension to Ruby's `Gemfile.lock`, but (A) the contents of the file are of a different MIME type than `Gemfile.lock` so it should be a different extension, and (B) `Gemfile.lock` is one of the worst, most baffling names ever given to an abstraction.  Other suggestions: `KnownGood.pipfile`, `ExactVersions.pipfile`, `Complete.pipfile`, `Exhaustive.pipfile`, `Repeatable.pipfile`...?

Don't get me wrong - this is extremely useful functionality and I'm looking forward to using it!  I just really hope that we can avoid repeating a templated set of mistakes that many programming tools seem intent on promoting these days.

issue_title: Incorrect nimbus value results in sparse submit exit without error message.
issue_body:
`sparse submit` exits without error message, only usage, if nimbus is set incorrectly to a non-empty value in config.json.

If I run the wordcount quickstart:

```
sparse quickstart wordcount
cd wordcount
sparse submit
```

I get a meaningful error: `error: No Nimbus server configured in config.json.`

When I set `"nimbus": "example"` in config.json, `sparse submit` shows the usage message without any error message:

```
usage: sparse [-h]
              {jar,kill,list,quickstart,remove_logs,run,stats,submit,tail,update_virtualenv,visualize,worker_uptime}
              ...

Utilities for managing Storm/streamparse topologies.
...
```

This raises questions about streamparse's config.json loading. It's a lazily loaded config, where values are pulled as needed via `get_env_config` in the source. We could consider a configuration object which loads all configuration early and validates it, also pointing out unknown keywords which were provided. This approach would give clear feedback to the user as to whether config.json is valid.

I am using sparse v2.0.2 on Python 2.7.10, source-compiled on Ubuntu 14.04 64-bit.

issue_title: downscale_local_mean with non-integer factors?
issue_body:
I'm interested in a version of `skimage.transform.downscale_local_mean()` which handles non-evenly dividing factors without padding the image. I want each pixel in the result to be an weighted average of overlapping pixels in the source, with weights equal to the area of overlap.

To be more explicit, this transformation is linear along equal axis. For example, from size 5 to size 2, it can be written as the matrix:
```
[[0.4, 0.4, 0.2, 0.0, 0.0],
```

Would this be a welcome addition to scikit-image in some form? I wrote a version that works for my own purposes, but my guess is that it's too slow for skimage, since I use a dense matrix multiply along each axis with matrix of weights.

issue_title: Connections limits
Add support for boolean parameters in Network API
issue_body:
If the datatype of a region is set to 'bool' in it's python implementation (a number of parameters in the old ImageSensor region are like this) a boolean value should be passable to the network api instead of `0` or `1`. The `YAMLUtils.cpp` tool for sending parameters between the python and cpp implementations does not currently support boolean values (see [_toScalar](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/master/src/nupic/engine/YAMLUtils.cpp#L47)). 

The current workaround for this can be seen in the python implementation for [SPRegion](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/nupic/regions/SPRegion.py#L230) where a `dataType=UInt32` is used `constraints=bool`. Instead of this workaround, dataType should support using `bool` as a type.

issue_title: Separate expiration interval for admin users
issue_body:
For security reasons, we'd like admin users (anyone on one of the admin site pages or anyone with `is_staff` set) to be logged out after 15 minutes of inactivity. Regular, unprivileged users aren't security-sensitive, so they could stay logged in for the default `SESSION_COOKIE_AGE`.

Could this feature be added to django-session-security? What's the best approach for doing so?

One possible idea would be to move the expiration-checking logic from `process_request` to `process_view`, so that the middleware can check the request's namespaces against some sort of `SESSION_SECURITY_SENSITIVE_NAMESPACES` list. If the sensitive namespaces list is non-empty, and `request.resolver_match.app_name` and/or `request.resolver_match.namespace` is NOT in the list, then the middleware would never logout.

PR #20 would also have addressed this, I think, but I see that it was reverted.

Or maybe there's another, better design. I'm pretty new to Django. I'm happy to help with implementation, if you're open to the idea and can give some guidance on a good design.

issue_title: BibRank: micro-optimize citation dict memory footprint
issue_body:
Originally on 2010-04-27

The citation dictionary is cached inside each WSGI Invenio daemon
process for speed purposes.  It looks like this: (for the demo site)

```
{18: [96],
 74: [92],
 77: [85, 86],
 78: [79, 91],
 79: [91],
 81: [82, 83, 87, 89],
 84: [85, 88, 91],
 91: [92],
 94: [80],
 95: [77, 86]}
```

For bigger sites containing 1M of records and having fuller citation
maps, this dictionary can get quite big, e.g. WSGI daemon processes of
the INSPIRE instance eat about 1 GB of RAM.

It would be good to decrease the memory footprint of this citation
dictionary, especially since we are running on a 64-bit OS, where we
may easily consume more bytes to store list elements (of `unsigned
mediumint' type) than necessary.

We should investigate potential local replacements for the list
structure, for example using `numpy.array`.  We can measure the
memory footprint of various data structures via `sys.getsizeof()`
or via `ps auxw` process sizes, aiming to find a more memory
optimized, yet still fast enough, data structure to represent the
citation dict.

If needed, we can even create a dedicated intbitset-like C extension,
that would be capable of storing recID vectors in a memory-efficient
way.  This is arguably the best micro-optimization technique that we
could go for, albeit it would represent a bit more work than reusing
`numpy.array` or other some such pre-existing module.

Note that this task is of a micro-optimization kind only, keeping the
overall citation indexer and searcher machinery unchanged, only
changing its internal data structures.  The tests will show how much
such a micro-optimization would be worth it.  The overall rethinking
of the citation dictionary handling and the inherent memory sharing
procedures would be another task, see some older musings at
[https://twiki.cern.ch/twiki/bin/view/CDS/InvenioScalability].

issue_title: RFC richer structure in commit log messages
issue_body:
## Background

Invenio commit log messages have standardised format described in:

- https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.rst (recommendation 6)
- http://invenio-software.org/wiki/Tools/Git/Workflow#R2.Remarksoncommitlogmessages

Besides better QA, better readability, easier future bisecting, etc,
this permits also to prepare release notes in an automated manner.

This works OK for small incremental update releases with 100+
commits:

- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/v1.1.4/RELEASE-NOTES

but it does not work so well for large feature releases consisting of 1000+
commits, as the recent v1.2.0 and v2.0.0 releases:

- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/v1.2.0/RELEASE-NOTES
- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/v2.0.0/RELEASE-NOTES

The problems include:

- Shortness of messages.  The commit message headline is limited to
  some 50 characters, which is good for being concise, however this is
  often not sufficient to express given change with reasonable
  clarity to the end users.

- Nature of changes.  We have started to use "NOTE" in the commit logs
  to attract attention to important (compatibility) changes, however
  this is not enough to inform the user about the nature of the
  change.  "Is it a new feature?  A bug fix?  Or just some internal
  refactoring that I don't have to care about?"

- One feature branch may consist of many commits, while for the
  reporting we may only need one item.  Some of the commits may be
  better "silenced" in the high-level end-user report.

## Proposal

Let's introduce richer structural markup in our commit log messages
that would address these drawbacks so that we could generate nicer,
more informative *progress reports* in between releases, and *release
notes* later.

Sphinx can serve as an example of what I have in mind:

- http://sphinx.readthedocs.org/en/latest/changes.html#release-1-2-3-released-sep-1-2014

There are basically two approaches how to address this: (1) via commit
log messages; (2) via tickets (issues, pull requests).

### Option 1: use richer structure in commit messages

We could use more leading labels besides NOTE, for example:

```
foo: better support for xyzzy and zyxxy

* FEATURE Foo now supports xyzzy in all the user facing web pages.

* FIX Corrects interface to Zyxxy...

* INCOMPATIBLE Removes foo...

Reviewed-by: John Doe
```

(Side note: instead of textual labels, we could use a subset of
[standard emojis](http://www.emoji-cheat-sheet.com/) that GitHub
supports, say ``:bug:`` instead of ``FIX``, bringing additional eye
candy to our commit logs.)

The richer commit log message structure could be checked by and
enforced via ``kwalitee`` tool that every programmer should run before
making pull requests anyway.

**Advantage:** git repository has all the information about our
software; this "preserves history"; it is usable offline; it is
independent of the ticketing system we use; it is close to
acked-by/reviewed-by directives.

**Disadvantage:** once committed, the information cannot be changed;
the integrators should pay extra attention and should promote
forward-thinking about formulating user-visible changes.

### Option 2: use ticket tracker

Everything we are dealing with has an issue on GitHub, and we triage
our issues with labels and milestones for release planning and
priorities:

- https://forum.invenio-software.org/t/triaging-issues-labels-milestones/58

Hence, instead of enriching the commit log message, we could store
release note like information in GitHub issues.  E.g. github issues
title could be used for item reporting, and could be made more verbose
that the commit log message headline.

Since our NOTE label contain verbose text to be displayed to users,
we'd have to store such a structured information an extra comments,
for example structured in YAML style:

```
notes:
  feature:
   - Foo now supports xyzzy in all the user facing web pages.
  fix:
   - Corrects interface to Zyxxy...
  incompatible_change:
   - Removes foo...
```

**Advantage:** messages can be easily edited after commit is done; "user
insignificant commits" can be grouped in one single issue/PR/branch;
usage of issues drives us to use tickets for everything, improving
visibility/milestone/planning.

**Disadvantage:** bound to concrete ticketing system; offline usage is
impossible; it is easy to "destroy history"; the notes are stored
outside of acked-by/tested-by/reviewed-by information (unless we
refactor this one too); extra information storage via issue comment
looks kind of ugly.

### Option 3: use git notes

We could use other techniques, such as git notes, where integrators
can store the further information about commits.  However, we've
[discussed that before](https://forum.invenio-software.org/t/usage-of-git-notes/55)
and found the usage of git notes not that attractive.

### WDYT?

Which of these directions do you prefer?

## Label set

Whatever technique we'll happen to prefer, we should muse in parallel
about the suitable label set to use.  

For example:

label         | meaning
--------------|-------------------------------------------
FEATURE       | commits adds a new feature
IMPROVEMENT   | commit improves an existing feature
REMOVAL       | commit removes some feature
FIX           | commit fixes a bug
DOCUMENTATION | no code, just docs
REFACTORING   | no user-facing changes
COMPATIBILITY | compatibility remarks, dropping Python-2.6
NOTE          | general note

issue_title: zdaemon transcript thread dies if disk is full
issue_body:
Two days ago I had a bit of a problem with a disk full.  Today my entire web server hung and stopped processing requests.

Long story short, when zdaemon's [Transcript thread](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zdaemon/blob/master/src/zdaemon/zdrun.py#L602) gets an IOError while writing to a log file, it just dies.  zdaemon itself and the child program it is managing remain running.  The child's stdout/stderr are now pointing to a pipe that is still open at both ends, but now no process ever reads from it.  Things seem to run fine for a couple of days, then the kernel's pipe buffer becomes full and the child blocks in write().  While holding the logging mutex.  Fun for the whole family, conveniently delayed from the root cause you think you already fixed.

issue_title: need more tests for the frontend
issue_body:
we need more tests for the frontend. I don't think we need to test every little piece of HTML, but it would be nice to have tests that at least exercise major features and ensure that there are no crashes. This would help us avoid breaking things when changing templatetags, etc. see e.g. #327 for an example of a change that broke a template different from the one that was being manually tested.

In principle it's enough to add more tests to ` test/frontend/test_basics.py`, just making sure the test sets up enough data to exercise major features in the templates, and that no crash happens.

I have a custom refresh serializer:

```
class CustomRefreshJSONWebTokenSerializer(RefreshJSONWebTokenSerializer):

        user_id = api_settings.JWT_PAYLOAD_GET_USER_ID_HANDLER(payload)

        if not user_id:
```

Is it possible to refresh token after username change?

issue_title: tarfile failure with long user ID
issue_body:
Note this is a follow-on from the same effort which produced #1012, but I think the bugs are independent.  I switched to Python 3 and the CrystaxNDK in an attempt to avoid NDK build errors, and got far enough to run into this.

While the dist's build.py is building its tar archive, I get `ValueError("overflow in number field")`:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/bin/p4a", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pythonforandroid/toolchain.py", line 754, in main
    ToolchainCL()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pythonforandroid/toolchain.py", line 343, in __init__
    getattr(self, command_method_name)(unknown)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pythonforandroid/toolchain.py", line 94, in wrapper_func
    func(self, args)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pythonforandroid/toolchain.py", line 589, in apk
    build_args = build.parse_args(args)
  File "/Users/testuser/.python-for-android/dists/unnamed_dist_1/build.py", line 488, in parse_args
    make_package(args)
  File "/Users/testuser/.python-for-android/dists/unnamed_dist_1/build.py", line 248, in make_package
    make_tar('assets/private.mp3', tar_dirs, args.ignore_path)
  File "/Users/testuser/.python-for-android/dists/unnamed_dist_1/build.py", line 197, in make_tar
    tf.add(fn, afn)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.5.2_3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/lib/python3.5/tarfile.py", line 1938, in add
    self.addfile(tarinfo, f)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.5.2_3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/lib/python3.5/tarfile.py", line 1960, in addfile
    buf = tarinfo.tobuf(self.format, self.encoding, self.errors)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.5.2_3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/lib/python3.5/tarfile.py", line 802, in tobuf
    return self.create_ustar_header(info, encoding, errors)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.5.2_3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/lib/python3.5/tarfile.py", line 821, in create_ustar_header
    return self._create_header(info, USTAR_FORMAT, encoding, errors)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.5.2_3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/lib/python3.5/tarfile.py", line 916, in _create_header
    itn(info.get("uid", 0), 8, format),
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.5.2_3/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.5/lib/python3.5/tarfile.py", line 211, in itn
    raise ValueError("overflow in number field")
ValueError: overflow in number field
```

It seems the overflow is in the "uid" field, and I notice that my UID on my Mac (part of a corporate domain) is pretty high at 1730938684.  Looking at the package in [`tarfile.py`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/tarfile.py#L218) it seems this is a limitation of the USTAR format which the python-4-android builder is selecting specifically.  The default GNU format can support much larger numbers, though I presume the unpacking code used in the Android package might not support it?

I was able to work around this problem by running as a different local user account, so long as that account owns both the .python-for-android directory and the source input directory.

Since UID information isn't relevant when unpacking on Android it would be nice to discard it and make this not matter, if using the GNU tar format isn't an option.  It doesn't look like the tarfile package gives the ability to do this directly, but you could chmod all the files to root (in a temporary location) before compressing them.

issue_title: Behaviour change of numexpr; NaN instead of 0
issue_body:
Hi,

I am not sure whether this is a `numexpr` issue, or rather a `mkl` or even a `numpy` issue. However, I think the most likely case is `numexpr`.

My pytest CI suite threw a lot of errors in my last run. Digging into it I could boil it down to this minimal example:

    import numpy as np
    from numexpr import evaluate
    test = np.array([-1-1j])*np.infty
    out1 = np.exp(test)
    out2 = evaluate("exp(test)")
    print(' PRINT OUT RESULT ::', out1, out2)

Doing this evaluation with `numpy`, hence `np.exp(test)`, results in `[ 0.-0.j]`. This result was also returned by `numexpr`, until recently. Now, `numexpr` returns `[nan+nanj]`.

I attach below the result for the above minimal example  (saved as `test_numexpr.py`). I run the test first with the up-to-date conda, where `numpy` and `numexpr` yield different results. Then I downgrade `mkl` from 2018 to 2017, which also slightly downgrades `numexpr`. With the older versions `numexpr` and `numpy` yield the same result:

    test/> python test_numexpr.py
    PRINT OUT RESULT :: [ 0.-0.j] [ nan+nanj]

    test/> conda install mkl=2017

    Fetching package metadata ...............
    Solving package specifications: .

    Package plan for installation in environment ~/anaconda3/envs/test_36:

    The following packages will be DOWNGRADED:

        mkl:     2018.0.0-hb491cac_4   --> 2017.0.4-h4c4d0af_0
        numexpr: 2.6.2-py36hdd3393f_1  --> 2.6.2-np113py36_0  
        numpy:   1.13.3-py36ha12f23b_0 --> 1.13.1-py36_0      
        scipy:   1.0.0-py36hbf646e7_0  --> 0.19.1-np113py36_0 

    test/> python test_numexpr.py
    PRINT OUT RESULT :: [ 0.-0.j] [ 0.-0.j]

Any ideas on that?

Thanks,
Dieter

issue_title: drawImage docstring confusion
issue_body:
I am finding myself tripping over the following paragraph from the docstring for GSObject.drawImage:

"Note that if you provide an `image` parameter, it is the image onto which the profile will be drawn.  The provided image *will be modified*.  A reference to the same image is also returned to provide parallel return behavior to when `image` is `None` (described above)."

My naive interpretation of this is that the following code should produce a fits file ('test_second_source.fits') with two sources on it

```
import galsim
import numpy

scale=0.2

blankImage = galsim.Image(int(200),int(200))

obj = galsim.Sersic(n=4, half_light_radius=0.14, flux=70000.0)
obj = obj.shear(q=0.75, beta=0.3*galsim.radians)
image1 = obj.drawImage(scale=scale, method='phot',image=blankImage)
image1.write('test_first_source.fits')


obj2 = galsim.Sersic(n=4, half_light_radius=0.14, flux=70000.0)
obj2 = obj2.shear(q=0.75, beta=0.3*galsim.radians)
dx = 0.25*(image1.xmax-image1.xmin)*scale
obj2=obj2.shift(dx,0.0)
image1 = obj2.drawImage(scale=scale,method='phot',image=image1)

image1.write('test_second_source.fits')
```
but that is not what happens.  test_second_source.fits only has one source in it.

Can someone please help me understand what I am doing wrong/not understanding?

Thanks.

issue_title: Chromatic Real Galaxy
issue_body:
GalSim has both `RealGalaxy` objects, and `ChromaticObject`s, but doesn't have `ChromaticRealGalaxy` (yet!).

@rmjarvis, @rmandelb, and I talked a bit about how one might take high-res HST images of a galaxy through 2+ filters, deconvolve the known (chromatic) HST PSF, and then reconvolve by a different chromatic PSF (to presumably less resolution than HST, c.f. `RealGalaxy`).  This would be especially helpful for studies of galaxies with color-gradients for LSST/Euclid/etc.

[Semboloni++13](http://adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2013MNRAS.432.2385S) section 5.2 equation 25 briefly lays this out for the case where the SED at each point in the galaxy is linear in wavelength.  We think we can generalize this to the case of generic SEDs, for instance, typical bulge and disk SEDs.

This issue is to develop the framework for a `ChromaticRealGalaxy` object.  For the moment, I think it's reasonable to limit the scope to just the framework.  In particular, this issue will not attempt to actually assemble a catalog of HST images and PSFs; we'll do that later, and in the meantime we can test with simulated GalSim images.

issue_title: --insecure || verify_ssl should be reflected when calling requests
issue_body:
The `verify_ssl` config option was added.  Currently, this only impacts whether ssl verification warnings are displayed.  It *should* also be used to inform `requests` whether to verify ssl upon connection.  

Currently, `tower-cli` defaults to `verify=False`.  The config option `verify_ssl` should influence whether `verify` is enabled || disabled when making `requests` calls.

issue_title: Consider using better health checking on Zookeeper and Consul.
issue_body:
Zookeeper has ephemeral nodes, and Consul has health checks.  Either of these mechanisms provides a means to detect master failure -- and even replica failure -- faster than via the polling method we need to use with etcd.

We should consider using this advanced mechanism on each of these SIS platforms.

issue_title: Check if a value passed inside body matches pattern described in `definitions`
issue_body:
### Description

According to Ron from swagger's IRC and http://editor.swagger.io/, this is a valid API configuration and it should check if passed value matches given pattern:

```yaml
---
swagger: '2.0'
info:
  version: 1.0.0
  title: Test API
paths:
  /:
    post:
      parameters:
        - in: body
          name: data
          schema:
            $ref: '#/definitions/Test'
      responses:
        200:
          description: OK
definitions:
  Test:
    type: string
    pattern: '^[01]{1,9}$'
```

Unfortunately, connexion does ignore pattern check with such API definition and lets user pass whatever he or she wants.

### Expected behaviour

Check if received values matches pattern defined in `definitions`.


### Actual behaviour

Connexion does not compare received value with pattern.


### Steps to reproduce

Use mentioned config with connexion, start server and call:

```
curl -X POST --header 'Content-Type: application/json' --header 'Accept: application/json' -d '"abcd"' 'http://0.0.0.0:8080/'
```

It should return an error, but it passes data to controller function instead.

Hi again,

I found out that if you don't include produces section you will get a very odd error:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/iwsmith/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1817, in wsgi_app
    response = self.full_dispatch_request()
  File "/Users/iwsmith/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1478, in full_dispatch_request
    response = self.make_response(rv)
  File "/Users/iwsmith/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1577, in make_response
    rv = self.response_class.force_type(rv, request.environ)
  File "/Users/iwsmith/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/werkzeug/wrappers.py", line 841, in force_type
    response = BaseResponse(*_run_wsgi_app(response, environ))
  File "/Users/iwsmith/anaconda/lib/python2.7/site-packages/werkzeug/test.py", line 867, in run_wsgi_app
    app_rv = app(environ, start_response)
TypeError: 'object' object is not callable
```

The swagger fragment that generated this is below:
```
/feedback/{transaction_id}:
    put:
      description: Notify Babel of actions taken based on a recommendations
      consumes:
      - application/json
      operationId: app.put_feedback
      parameters:
        - in: path
```

issue_title: Should have ability to read LookupParameters as matrix

This is not currently supported, but should be easy to add by allowing the `parameter()` expression to take LookupParameters as input as well.

issue_title: Parameter collection behaviour
issue_body:
Not sure if this is a bug or if I'm doing something wrong but I'm having some trouble understanding the behaviour of the parameter collection object.
I have a network object with a parameter collection (self.model) which I pass to 2 MLPs. The MLPs are used for scoring during training. They are defined as their own object.
If I make the MLPs their own parameter collection, my network trains fine but otherwise it does not.
It seems to create the parameters all right (the saved model file looks fine) but the loss and errors don't go down. I guess it means that the MLP parameters don't get backpropped into.
The MLP parameters used to be defined directly inside my Network object, but I wanted to move them out into their own file to make the code easier to work with.
I can go back to that ugly version but I won't if I can avoid :)
I can make the MLPs their own parameter collection but it will be a bit more of a pain to do model saving and loading.

Any idea what might be going on?

This is a skeleton of what my code looks like:

```python
import dynet as dy

class Network(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.model = dy.ParameterCollection()
        self.trainer = dy.AdamTrainer(self.model)
        self.mlp1 = MLP(self.model, in_dim, hid_dim, out_dim1)
        self.mlp2 = MLP(self.model, in_dim, hid_dim, out_dim2)

    def train():
        for example in examples:
            score1 = self.mlp1(example)
            score2 = self.mlp2(example)
            loss = loss_function(score1,score2)
        self.trainer.update()


class MLP(object):
    def __init__(self, model, in_dim, hid_dim, out_dim, activation=dy.tanh):
        #model = dy.ParameterCollection() # this works
        self._W1 = model.add_parameters((hid_dim, in_dim))
        self._b1 = model.add_parameters(hid_dim)
        self._W2 = model.add_parameters((out_dim, hid_dim))
        self._b2 = model.add_parameters(out_dim)
        self.activation = activation

    def hid_layer(self,x):
        W = dy.parameter(self._W1)
        b = dy.parameter(self._b1)
        return self.activation(W*x+b)

    def out_layer(self,x):
        W = dy.parameter(self._W2)
        b = dy.parameter(self._b2)
        return self.activation(W*x+b)

    def __call__(self,x):
        h = self.hid_layer(x)
        return self.out_layer(h)
```

Hello,

From [this page](https://blogs.adobe.com/CCJKType/2009/02/using_afdko_to_build_opentype_1.html), `mergeFonts` can be used to *"merge multiple fonts, add/remove glyphs, and change glyph names"*. issue_title: datastore: Query does not allow inequality filter on __key__ property.
issue_body:
Datastore query raises a ValueError for inequality(non-equality to be correct) operators on `__key__` property. 
https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/3e86aaed3c7e192a17d9b790f90a41cc2b7baa6a/gcloud/datastore/query.py#L223

According to Google Cloud Datastore documentation inquality operators on `__key__` property are supported, and should not raise exception.
https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs/concepts/queries#Datastore_Key_filters

It is a critical part of our data-model.

issue_title: Cloud spanner client does not seem to be retrying broken streams
issue_body:
[Documentation](http://google-cloud-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/spanner/snapshot-usage.html) for snapshot transactions mentions:

```
If streaming a chunk fails due to a “resumable” error, Session.read() retries the StreamingRead API reqeust, passing the resume_token from the last partial result streamed.
```

This is the correct thing to do but the client actually does not seem to be doing this. I looked around a little bit in the code and I could find anyplace where it is retrying the read with the resume token. 
This is the place where the stream would throw an error if it broke:
https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/9d9b6c0708ae6553458ad6107e5ad8efb23762e8/spanner/google/cloud/spanner/streamed.py#L132

 I dont see any exception handling happening here and it looks like that the error would just be propagated back to the user.

Notice where the datastore specific variables need to be set for the example to work.

```python
from main import main

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

`main.py`:

```python
import os

from google.cloud import datastore

CREDENTIALS = 'YOUR-JSON-FILENAME-HERE'
PROJECT = 'YOUR-PROJECT-NAME-HERE'
NAMESPACE = 'YOUR-NAMESPACE-HERE'
KIND = 'YOUR-KIND-HERE'

PROJECT_DIR = os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__))
os.environ['GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS'] = \
    os.path.join(PROJECT_DIR, CREDENTIALS)
ds_client = datastore.Client(project=PROJECT, namespace=NAMESPACE)

keys = []
query = ds_client.query(kind=KIND)
for entity in query.fetch():
    keys.append(entity.key.name)


def main():
    print('\n'.join(keys))
```

This will *always* fail with the following stacktrace:

```

    return super(GAPICDatastoreAPI, self).run_query(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/contextlib.py", line 35, in __exit__
    self.gen.throw(type, value, traceback)
  File "/home/user/src/consult/test/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/google/cloud/datastore/_gax.py", line 82, in _catch_remap_gax_error
    six.reraise(error_class, new_exc, sys.exc_info()[2])
  File "/home/user/src/consult/test/venv/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/google/cloud/datastore/_gax.py", line 74, in _catch_remap_gax_error

    return self._run_query(request, options)
```

issue_title: Implement DB-API 2.0 driver for Spanner
issue_body:
Per [PEP 249](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/).

- `connect()` module-scope function would return an instance implementing the `Connection` interface.
- Each `SpannerConnection` class would hold a `Session` instance.
- `SpannerConnection` and `SpannerCursor` would work together to map the DB-API semantics onto snapshots, batches, transactions, etc.

Note that until the back-end supports DML, there is no feasible way to do a proper DB-API mapping.

issue_labels: [{'id': 139685153, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20pubsub', 'name': 'api: pubsub', 'color': 'f8f8f8', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604243, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p2', 'name': 'priority: p2', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438580, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': 'd93f0b', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
I recently upgraded to google-cloud-pubsub 0.33.1 and it looks like `future.result()` is not raising callback exceptions how it used to. Here's the code I'm using:
```python
from google.cloud import pubsub_v1
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

subscriber = pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient()
flow_control = pubsub_v1.types.FlowControl() 
subscription_path = subscriber.subscription_path('project.name', 'sub.name')
executor = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1)
policy = pubsub_v1.subscriber.policy.thread.Policy(
    subscriber, subscription_path, executor=executor, flow_control=flow_control
)     

def callback(message):
    print('got message:', message)
    raise ValueError('test')

future = policy.open(callback)
while True:
    print('Start of error loop')
    try:
        future.result()
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print('Got interrupt, exiting!')
        break
    except Exception as e:
        print('Got uncaught error in callback!')
        print(e)

policy.close()
```

As you can see, `callback` raises a ValueError after printing the message. However, the `future.result` call in the `try` block never raises this exception. Any idea how to get this error?

issue_title: instance_setup should generate SSH host keys when none are present
issue_body:
Right now, instance_setup does not correctly handle the case where no SSH host keys are present, failing to generate them and preventing SSH from starting.

Instead, when instance_setup triggers a key (re)generation, it could work this way:

1. If instance_config specifies a list of cipher suites for which to generate host keys, generate that list.
1. Otherwise, if any host keys are present on the system, generate that list.
1. Otherwise, unconditionally generate ed25519, ecdsa, and rsa host keys. Don't generate dsa keys in this case, as they're no longer viewed as secure; for example, Debian 9 no longer generates these keys upon a fresh install.

In terms of evaluating success vs failure, a success would be "at least one freshly generated host key is created, and no pre-existing host key remains on the system." If no host keys end up on the disk, it willl be impossible to log in, and if any old host keys remain present, there may be a security issue.

I suggest logging a non-blocking debugging message if any attempted cipher types fail to generate, but this is not inherently a problem by itself.

issue_title: ICMP Secure Redirects and CIS7 Compliance
issue_body:
Running into a compliance issue with a couple of the network kernel params being set for GCP images.

The the kernel networking parameters for allowing secure ICMP redirects, `net.ipv4.conf.all.secure_redirects` & `net.ipv4.conf.default.secure_redirects`, are being enabled on GCP RHEL7 images (lines 31-32, [`/sysctl/11-gce-network-security.conf`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/compute-image-packages/blob/e843cc6919bbed8203ef8ce87b5a3120d4e86800/google_config/sysctl/11-gce-network-security.conf#L31-L32)).   The Common Internet Security Benchmark for RHEL7 (CIS7) indicates they should be disabled (4.2.3 - pg. 67, [CIS_Red_Hat_Enterprise_Linux_7_Benchmark_v1.0.0.pdf](https://benchmarks.cisecurity.org/tools2/linux/CIS_Red_Hat_Enterprise_Linux_7_Benchmark_v1.0.0.pdf)).

Is there a specific reason why GCP need to allow ICMP redirects from secure gateways (i.e., is there any issue if we disable them for compliance)?

The rationale for disabling them, from the CIS7 benchmark:
> It is still possible for even known gateways to be compromised. Setting net.ipv4.conf.all.secure_redirects to 0 protects the system from routing table
updates by possibly compromised known gateways.

- [broken dependencies argument](https://www.pantsbuild.org/3rdparty_jvm.html#your-codes-build-file)
- [assumes pathspec library exists in requirements.txt file](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/examples/src/python/example/3rdparty_py.md#3rdpartypython)
- [code snippet assumes call to register() ends with `help=` argument](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/docs/docs.md#goals-tasks-and-options)

I think these kinds of issues can be resolved by removing inline code snippets and exclusively using `!inc` directives whenever possible. This takes two parts:

1. All `!inc` directives using `start-at`/`start-before`/`end-at`/`end-after` parameters should refer to specific comments marking the start and end of the section of code that should be included. If these markers are not found, pants should throw an error.
2. For files which we don't need to show any specific section of and don't want to add comments to (e.g. `requirements.txt`), add a `lines` parameter to `!inc` and use it instead of including the whole file, or assuming some specific string is contained in it. (e.g. `!inc[lines=1-12](3rdparty/python/requirements.txt)`.

An example of what adding "specific comments" could look like (the first `!inc` change) :
`src/python/pants/core_tasks`:
``` python
  # register-options-help-parameter:start
  @classmethod
  def register_options(cls, register):
    super(ListGoals, cls).register_options(register)
    register('--graph', type=bool,
             help='Generate a graphviz graph of installed goals.')
    register('--all', type=bool,
             help='List all goals even if no description is available.')
  # register-options-help-parameter:end
```
`src/docs/docs.md`:
``` markdown
!inc[start-after=register-options-help-parameter:start&end-before=register-options-help-parameter:end](../python/pants/core_tasks/list_goals.py)
```

The above example is a very simple solution that wouldn't require changing how `!inc` works (except for throwing if the comments are not found). If we wanted to make it more streamlined, we could ensure that each comment string (e.g. `register-options-help-parameter` in the above example). We could also consider requiring the `:start` and `:end` parts to mark ranges of a file if that convention makes sense.

There are instances of code snippets such as example shell commands which we don't have any instance of in the repository to include from (e.g. [here](https://www.pantsbuild.org/first_tutorial.html#help)) -- in these cases, we could just leave them as inline snippets. However, pulling examples from the repo seems to be the most robust way to reduce outdated documentation going forward. I have made a lot of the edits mentioned above to test this idea out privately and have been able to switch over almost every literal code snippet to an `!inc` call, all without making any changes to the codebase except adding comments.

I can put up a pull request making the `!inc` changes asap if this sounds useful. Let me know if I can be more specific about any of this.

issue_title: Implement deferred sources using ivy in the new engine

Deferred sources are a particularly isolated case of ivy usage, and should make a good first test case. Their current implementation is also quite awkward (multiple tasks and mutability in Target to inject sources later).

This is blocked-by-but-related-to #2978.

issue_title: Introducing O(log(n)) from O(n) by allowing batch requests
issue_body:
The current API and Google Maps server only allows processing of one address at a time. We can increase the efficiency of the API by allowing batch requests which reduces the theoretical time complexity from O(n) to O(log(n)) which can be validated using applied performance tests that I have conducted below,

![performance](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/11673956/36493519-cf893794-16fd-11e8-82a3-ef49d38111f5.png)

As we can see, allowing even a batch size of 11 reduced the runtime from ~1216 seconds to ~123 seconds or a ~9.88 fold increase in efficiency. This can decrease the load of both clients and servers.

issue_title: Unicode objects with non-ascii characters throw UnicodeEncodingError exception (instead of RepeatedLabelError)
issue_body:
This code is only a couple dozen lines long, so how is it possible it has a bug? Unicode, that's how.

When this error is thrown and the input is a unicode object containing non-ascii characters, this code throws something like this:

```
Error
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/mlissner/Programming/intellij/courtlistener/cl/lib/tests.py", line 554, in test_normalize_atty_contact
    result = normalize_attorney_contact(pair['q'])
  File "/home/mlissner/Programming/intellij/courtlistener/cl/lib/pacer.py", line 439, in normalize_attorney_contact
    tag_mapping=mapping,
  File "/home/mlissner/.virtualenvs/courtlistener/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/usaddress/__init__.py", line 178, in tag
    label)
  File "/home/mlissner/.virtualenvs/courtlistener/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/probableparsing/__init__.py", line 22, in __init__
    repo_url=self.REPO_URL)
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\xe9' in position 55: ordinal not in range(128)
```

This is unfortunate because my calling code is designed to catch RepeatedLabelErrors not UnicodeEncodeErrors (and ideally it'd stay that way).

I guess the solution is to assume that people are going to use unicode within their code instead of strings, and to tweak the code by adding a `u` before the definition of `MESSAGE` and `DOCS_MESSAGE`. When I do that, it fixes the bug.

What I haven't thought through is what happens if people are using strings in the parser instead of using unicode objects. In that case, do things get better or worse?

issue_title: Does pylint warning no-else-return make sense to you
issue_body:
Hi fellow SoCo developers

With the new version of pytlint:
```text
pylint 1.7.1, 
astroid 1.5.2
```

a warning was introduced about an else clause on a if statement after a return statement. I cannot seem to find documentation for this from pylint, but it seems to follow the Chromiums guide lines, at mentioned in this stack overflow question: http://stackoverflow.com/a/28250521.

Basically, it seems that pylint now enforces this style:
```python
if whatever:
    return something
return something_else
```
over this one
```python
if whatever:
    return something
else:
    return something_else
```

While I usually find that I agree with pylint on most things, I cannot say that I do on this one. While obviously identical, I think that while the first form would work for me, the second form is slightly more readable, and therefore I definitely do not think it should discouraged. I have disabled the warning for now, but I would very much like input on the matter.

issue_title: Add an option to filter fasta by line number
issue_body:
Make it so `filter-fasta.py` can be given a file name of (sorted) line numbers and then only print those lines to output. This will be useful for fast(er) filtering of JSON and FASTA input to `noninteractive-alignment-panel.py`. That fast filtering would then be done in the following steps:

```sh
$ bzcat files*.json.bz2 | grep -n pattern > temp
$ cut -f1 -d: < temp > line-numbers
$ cut -f2- -d: | bzip2 > new.json.bz2
$ filter-fasta.py --printLines line-numbers > new.fasta
```

(The two `cut` calls could be handled by one program too if we want).

The idea here is that we can grep for some things (like a subject title) quickly in the `json.bz2` files without having to parse the JSON on each line (or even use Python at all). Then we get the line numbers and pull the corresponding sequences out of the FASTA (`noninteractive-alignment-panel.py` needs both the JSON and the FASTA).

The reason for providing line numbers in a file is that if we have a billion line numbers we want, the shell will break if they're all on the command line. Reading them from a file is no problem.

GoBGP creates numeric operators according to the following table(written in [draft-ietf-idr-rfc5575bis-06](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-idr-rfc5575bis-06#section-4.2.3)):
```
            +----+----+----+----------------------------------+
            | lt | gt | eq | Resulting operation              |
            +----+----+----+----------------------------------+
            | 0  | 0  | 0  | true (independent of the value)  |
            | 0  | 0  | 1  | == (equal)                       |

            | 1  | 0  | 0  | < (less than)                    |

            | 1  | 1  | 0  | != (not equal value)             |
            | 1  | 1  | 1  | false (independent of the value) |
            +----+----+----+----------------------------------+
```

Is ExaBGP implemented to decode these?

##### ISSUE TYPE
performance enhancement

```
3.4.18
```

##### ENVIRONMENT 
```

```
##### CONFIGURATION
```

```
##### SUMMARY
Sending route updates over the API slows down as the RIB-out grows for AF vpnv6. With an empty RIB-out I measured a rate ~750/second. When the RIB is at 100K updates are processed at 1/second.
Conversely, for vpnv4 I measured ~1200/sec with the empty RIB and up to 100K entries, no noticeable slowdown.

##### STEPS TO REPRODUCE
Define at least one peer for the AF under test and measure the rate of processing routes injected through the API and sent towards the peer.


##### EXPECTED RESULTS
I would expect the performance to be closer between the AFs vpnv4 and vpnv6, understanding that vpnv6 will cost more to process. 

##### ACTUAL RESULTS


##### IMPORTANCE
Blocker for introducing the software in a production environment for AF vpnv6.

issue_title: get_example_from_prop_spec doesn't support empty properties
issue_body:
If you have something like `additionalProperties: {}` is defined `get_example_from_prop_spec` will call `_example_from_complex_def` ([here](https://github.com/Trax-air/swagger-parser/blob/master/swagger_parser/swagger_parser.py#L175)), which will assume a `schema` is defined. 

Note: `additionalProperties: {}` is useful to allow any additional properties without an explicit schema (is equals to JSON schema `true`).

issue_title: MLE parameter estimation for Markov Network
ttf build from UFO does not include name table platformID = 1 records
issue_body:
fontmake v1.3.0 from PyPI
OS X 10.12.5
Python v3.6.2
UFO v2 source files

When I build ttf from UFO with fontmake using the following:

```
$ fontmake -u Hack-Regular.ufo
```

the name table includes only platformID = 3 / encodingID = 1 / languageID = 1033 records. 

![platformid_fontmake](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4249591/30033105-3e957da4-9167-11e7-86af-ab857e0189c2.png)

Validation tests in Font Book seem to indicate that the current version of OS X is happy without platformID = 1 name records:

![osx_test](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4249591/30033126-5a1f1e5e-9167-11e7-9e39-494f0422aa51.png)

however [Apple documentation](https://developer.apple.com/fonts/TrueType-Reference-Manual/RM06/Chap6name.html) seems to indicate otherwise:

> The supported platform identifier (platformID) codes are given in the following table. PlatformID codes have been assigned for Unicode, Macintosh, and Microsoft. PlatformID codes 240 through 255 have been reserved for user-defined platforms and are not available for registration. Names with platformIDs other than 0, 1, and 2 are ignored on OS X and iOS.

Are the platformID = 1 tables necessary for OS X users (including past releases of OS X) and, if so, is it possible to include these by default with fontmake builds?

When a course page on the old site redirects to the new site, the old_style download attempts to parse it and fails, but not raising the exception that causes the on_demand try. An example is the `crypto` course.


Applying this banal patch to force the on-demand download works:

```
diff --git a/coursera/coursera_dl.py b/coursera/coursera_dl.py
index a6672b2..6e359e2 100644
--- a/coursera/coursera_dl.py
+++ b/coursera/coursera_dl.py

     Returns True if the class appears completed.

-        return download_old_style_class(args, class_name)
-    except ClassNotFound:
-        logging.debug('Downloading new style (on demand) class %s', class_name)
-        return download_on_demand_class(args, class_name)
+    return download_on_demand_class(args, class_name)
 
 
 def main():
```

issue_title: Having all attributes be flat on Session makes API hard to use.
issue_body:
From Michael:

Properties in our LabVIEW property nodes have a hierarchy:

![2017-12-03-094303_694x526_scrot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8085768/33678983-15cf34ae-da83-11e7-97ae-ef9043331ec6.png)

This makes it easy for users to discover which attributes are available and how they fit into the overall picture, while hiding irrelevant details.
 
In comparison, the Python experience is pretty poor:

![2017-12-03-102032_1354x1438_scrot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8085768/33679034-387d65f2-da83-11e7-90b3-33e40e427e96.png)

Not only does the completion list have so many entries that it won't fit on my screen, in this case the first completion returned is a fairly advanced synchronization attribute (which only makes sense with TClk), and the next two apply only to users of the PXI-5922 + PXI-5900 combo. The vast majority of users don't care about these attributes, which is why they're so deep in the hierarchy:
Clocking
Advanced
Absolute Sample Clock Offset
Device
Accessory
Gain
Offset
 
I think there needs to be more hierarchy here, otherwise this is very difficult for users.

issue_title: ApplicationSession can't load objects from postgresql database through django ORM
issue_body:
Hello,

I write a python (3.4) project using django. 
This project is launched from supervisor.
Crossbar is used as "customer" and components are connected to a crossbar instance (as router) on another server. 
Crossbar is also launched by supervisor.
All run in a virtualenv.

Config file has only one worker as "container" and multiple component which are defined in the django project as ApplicationSession.

config.json example :
```
{
  "version": 2,
  "workers": [
    {
      "type": "container",
      "options": {
        "pythonpath": [
          ".."
        ]
      },
      "components": [
        {
          "type": "class",
          "realm": "realm1",
          "classname": "myproject.crossbar.MyAppSession",
          "transport": {
            "type": "websocket",
            "endpoint": {
              "type": "tcp",
              "host": "server.com",
              "port": 8080
            },
            "url": "ws://server.com:8080/ws"
          }
        },
        ...
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```


All ApplicationSessions defined as component push data every seconds. Very simple.
Every ApplicationSession looks like this :

```
from autobahn.twisted.wamp import ApplicationSession
from autobahn.twisted.util import sleep
from twisted.internet.defer import inlineCallbacks

class MyAppSession(ApplicationSession):

    @inlineCallbacks
    def onJoin(self, details):
        while True:
            try:
                data = {} # send fake data
                self.publish("myappsession",  data)
                print(data)
                yield sleep(1)
            except:
                continue
```

While ApplicationSessions do not access to the database, all work fine.
I had a third Crossbar instance as client to check incoming data. All is good.

Now i want send data saved in postgresql database.
I just add few line like this :

```
import django
django.setup() # absolutely necessary, else crossbar error : django.core.exceptions.AppRegistryNotReady: Apps aren't loaded yet. 
...
from autobahn.twisted.wamp import ApplicationSession
from autobahn.twisted.util import sleep
from twisted.internet.defer import inlineCallbacks
from myproject.models import MyObject

class MyAppSession(ApplicationSession):

    @inlineCallbacks
    def onJoin(self, details):
        while True:
            try:
                data = {} # send fake data
                for d in MyObject.objects.all():
                     data.append({"id": d.id,
                                  "name": d.name})
                self.publish("myappsession",  data)
                print(data)
                yield sleep(1)
            except:
                continue
```

Restart Crossbar and  ... BOOOOM

Crossbar cpu grow up until 100%
Any data is send.
Third instance of crossbar receive nothing (logic). 
htop command show me two postgresql process locked (idle and SELECT)

Crossbar "infinite" log :
`WAMP message serialization error: cannot serialize unknown object: <generator object myobjects at 0x7487ae90>`


Asked previsouly on [Crossbar github](https://github.com/crossbario/crossbar/issues/869#event-778589624)

Any help will be appreciate
Regards

issue_title: Feature request: support pulling data from AWS S3 buckets directly (using boto3)
issue_body:
Sometimes we have to pull data files from a private S3 bucket, which requires AWS credentials, so we can't just access it with a simple HTTP GET request. To handle this, we could use `boto3` or the `awscli` to pull s3 files when the file is specified by an s3 path, like `s3://path-to-file`, instead of the http url. At this time it seems that the alternative is to just manually download the files locally first using the `awscli` or `boto3` and then point allennlp to the local copy.

The only downside I can see of supporting this feature is adding an additional dependency (`boto3`).

I'd be willing to submit a PR if this is something you want to support.

issue_title: _get_unicode with force=True breaks with unicode characters on Python 2.7
issue_body:
On Python 2.7, the `_get_unicode` function on `line_protocol.py` breaks if the passed text contains an Unicode code point with a `UnicodeEncodeError` if `force` is True. 

For example:
```python
Python 2.7.6 (default, Mar 22 2014, 22:59:56) 
Type "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.

In [1]: from influxdb import line_protocol

In [2]: line_protocol._get_unicode(u'ç')
Out[2]: u'\xe7'

In [3]: line_protocol._get_unicode(u'ç', force=True)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
UnicodeEncodeError                        Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-3-fb06071b454e> in <module>()
----> 1 line_protocol._get_unicode(u'ç', force=True)

/<virtualenv>/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/influxdb/line_protocol.pyc in _get_unicode(data, force)
     75         return ''
     76     elif force:
---> 77         return str(data)
     78     else:
     79         return data
```
I worked around it by substituting `return str(data)` with `return unicode(data)`. I can't really say what is the full impact of this change, but this seemed to work so far.

This problem shows up when I try to generate a line when one of its tags has a string with Unicode characters:

```python
  File "<virtualenv>/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/influxdb/client.py", line 373, in write_points
    tags=tags)

    expected_response_code=204

    data=make_lines(data, precision).encode('utf-8'),
  File "<virtualenv>/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/influxdb/line_protocol.py", line 108, in make_lines
    value = _escape_tag(tags[tag_key])
  File "<virtualenv>/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/influxdb/line_protocol.py", line 40, in _escape_tag
    tag = _get_unicode(tag, force=True)
  File "<virtualenv>/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/influxdb/line_protocol.py", line 77, in _get_unicode
    return str(data)
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\xe9' in position 9: ordinal not in range(128)
```

issue_title: Exception message verbosity for <model>.DoesNotExist exception
issue_body:
Exception message verbosity for related (and failed) lookups could be enhanced by adding the source object's type, identity and, if set, the querytime used for the lookup.
Note that the DoesNotExist class is bound to the related model's class.
So, the following code would raise an ``Article.DoesNotExist`` exception if no article is found:
```
class Journal(Versionable):
   name = CharField(...)

class Article(Versionable):
   name = CharField(...)
   journal = VersionedForeignKey(Journal)

# ...some code happens here...

# Assume there's no current journal assigned to article, this would raise a Journal.DoesNotExist exception saying: "DoesNotExist: Journal matching query does not exist."
journal = article.journal
```

issue_title: Check running platform version
issue_body:
Platform version is stored in a number of ways throughout Deis.  Checking the version at different stages in a cluster's life cycle varies. A source of truth should be defined to know if an upgrade is needed or if a upgrade was successful.

## Current state of versioning

`/etc/deis-release` is set on initial install but not updated to reflect running version if a platform upgrade is performed.
https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/contrib/coreos/user-data.example#L102-L104

Platform version is set for performing an upgrade.  This is a `desired` version and does not reflect what is running.
http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/upgrading-deis/#in-place-upgrade


## Proposal

Add component versions to `deisctl version` to check container `DEIS_RELEASE` environment variables.

```
Client:
    deisctl:        v1.10.1
API:
    controller:     v1.7
Platform:
	builder:        v1.10.1
	controller:     v1.10.1
	database:       v1.10.1
```

issue_title: Standalone RPC test harness
Using F() expressions?
Pred hashing not consistent with Pred equality
issue_body:
The Pred component class compares with strings that may or may not end in `_rel`:

```python
>>> p = Pred.stringpred('udef_q_rel')
>>> p == 'udef_q'
True
>>> p == 'udef_q_rel'
True
>>> p == '_udef_q'
False
>>> p == 'udef'
False
```

But this does not work with hashing (e.g. in a dict or set):

```python
>>> Pred.stringpred('udef_q') in set(['udef_q', 'proper_q'])
True
>>> Pred.stringpred('udef_q_rel') in set(['udef_q', 'proper_q'])
False
>>> Pred.stringpred('udef_q') in set(['udef_q_rel', 'proper_q'])
False
>>> Pred.stringpred('udef_q') in set([Pred.stringpred('udef_q_rel'), Pred.stringpred('proper_q')])
False
>>> 'udef_q' in set([Pred.stringpred('udef_q_rel'), Pred.stringpred('proper_q')])
False
>>> 'udef_q_rel' in set([Pred.stringpred('udef_q_rel'), Pred.stringpred('proper_q')])
True
>>> any(Pred.stringpred('udef_q_rel') == q for q in set(['udef_q', 'proper_q']))
True
```

Hashing consistently on the short form would help when a Pred object is checked against Pred objects in a hash, but not when strings are used. I can't think of a good way around the latter issue, so maybe some documentation needs to be written about that.

I need to get anaconda to install packages not in the home/user directory because it doesn't have enough space to hold all the packages.

Solutions I've tried.
	- Create environment using the --prefix specifier

	- Create Symlinks that connect /home/user/.conda to /opt/anaconda/.conda

                         	"CondaError: No writable package cache directories found in
	                         ('/opt/anaconda/3-4.3.1/pkgs', '/home/user/.conda/pkgs')"
			*Can still access the environments, just can't use conda create or conda install
		○ Tried editing .condarc to allow the use of softlinks.
		○ Tried using both absolute and relative symlinks
	- Edit .condarc file to specify /opt/anaconda/.conda/envs as the environment directory
		○ envs_dirs:

issue_title: pip installs incompatible package in python 3 enviornment
issue_body: This happens with the [gevent](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/gevent/1.0.2) package. Even though `gevent` is incompatible with python 3, running `pip install gevent` in a python 3 environment installs it which later leads to unexpected syntax errors.
