issue_title: Conda always creating pyc files for noarch: python packages
issue_body:
For packages containing python files, it is possible to prevent automatic creation of pyc files during conda build processing by using skip_compile_pyc.

Unfortunately, for packages declared noarch: python, pyc files are created during the link step unconditionally.

Ideally, this would be controlled by the same patterns set for skip_compile_pyc at build time.

And here is the relevant segment of the `appveyor.yml` in question:

issue_title: support for package containers (eg. wheel)
issue_body:
If one installs conda to a network filesystem, imports take a long time, because the installation method of conda enforces unpacking everything. This problem has already been solved by the wheel and egg zip file formats, which avoid the overhead of reading thousands of individual files. The Java world has jars for this purpose. So I got some questions.
* Is there any good reason to enforce unpacking wheels in the first place?
* Is wheel interfering with the meta-data concept of conda in any way? Duplicating the info wouldn't be a problem, would it?
* Do you think it would be hard to implement? (eg. just omit the unzip step and just copy the archive file to site-packages)

Thanks in advance for your feedback on this. I hope it is not a duplicate (githubs search feature is kinda limited, sorry).

issue_title: Newest HDF5 package doesn't link on Windows
issue_body:
After updating HDF5 to version 1.8.16 (specifically, https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/win-64/hdf5-1.8.16-vc14_0.tar.bz2), I get link errors like
```
unresolved external symbol H5T_NATIVE_INT_g 
unresolved external symbol H5T_NATIVE_DOUBLE_g 
unresolved external symbol H5P_CLS_DATASET_CREATE_ID_g
```
The problem is a missing line in a configuration header: Since HDF5 was compiled into a DLL, the file `include/H5pubconf.h` should contain
```
#define H5_BUILT_AS_DYNAMIC_LIB 1
```
This line used to be present in version 1.8.15.1 (specificaly, https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/win-64/hdf5-1.8.15.1-vc14_4.tar.bz2), but is missing in the new package version. The HDF5 documentation claims that the line in question is automatically inserted by cmake, so this looks like an error in Continuum's build scripts. Please correct!

issue_title: Why need PrefixReplaceLinkAction? It breaks Windows permissions.
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [x] bug report - [ ] feature request ## Current Behavior If the conda environment is read-only to Users, installing as admin packages with files that have "prefix_placeholder" overwrite these files permissions to have no access to Users and makes these packages unusable. ### Steps to Reproduce 1. There is a base installation of Anaconda in C:\Anaconda with permissions inherited from C:\: * Administrators read/write * Users read-only 2. However, C:\Anaconda\Library\Qt5Core.dll permissions are different and explicit: * Administrators read/write * Administrator user read/write * Users - no access 3. `conda uninstall qt` and `conda install qt` as administrator user do not change anything 4. Upon further investigation it turns out that in C:\Anaconda\pkgs\qt-5.9.6-vc14h62aca36_0\info\paths.json the entry for Qt5Core.dll has prefix placeholder: ``` { "_path": "Library/bin/Qt5Core.dll", "file_mode": "binary", "path_type": "hardlink", "prefix_placeholder": "C:/qt5/_h_env", "sha256": "50886da211b4acaacc015e18f52fe44c2a07500f05fd09a9cb3a5c026a5eaba4", "size_in_bytes": 5241344 }, ``` 5. The TRACE output of `conda install qt` shows that in this case PrefixReplaceLinkAction is used: ``` TRACE conda.core.path_actions:verify(385): copying C:\Anaconda\pkgs\qt-5.9.6-vc14h62aca36_0\Library\bin\Qt5Core.dll => C:\Users\labdsvm-admin\tmp6f8xpxgo\a39281e8-cb0c-4f01-9d32-e22b2209f393 TRACE conda.gateways.disk.create:_do_copy(275): copying C:\Anaconda\pkgs\qt-5.9.6-vc14h62aca36_0\Library\bin\Qt5Core.dll => C:\Users\labdsvm-admin\tmp6f8xpxgo\a39281e8-cb0c-4f01-9d32-e22b2209f393 TRACE conda.core.path_actions:verify(390): rewriting prefixes in C:\Anaconda\Library\bin\Qt5Core.dll ``` 6. The temp directory is created by mkdtemp() and the [documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/tempfile.html#tempfile.mkdtemp) says that > The directory is readable, writable, and searchable only by the creating user ID. 7. This operation first copies the file from pkgs folder to temporary folder that is only writable by current user and later it hardlinks the file from this directory into C:\Anaconda\Library\bin. ``` TRACE conda.core.path_actions:execute(415): linking C:\Users\labdsvm-admin\tmp6f8xpxgo\a39281e8-cb0c-4f01-9d32-e22b2209f393 => C:\Anaconda\Library\bin\Qt5Core.dll TRACE conda.gateways.disk.create:create_link(321): hard linking C:\Users\labdsvm-admin\tmp6f8xpxgo\a39281e8-cb0c-4f01-9d32-e22b2209f393 => C:\Anaconda\Library\bin\Qt5Core.dll ``` 8. The hardlinks retain source file's permissions, so now, the DLL is not readable by other Users. ## Expected Behavior I do not understand why the treatment for files with prefix_placeholder is so radically different. Why conda cannot hardlink the file from pkgs folder as other files? Why copy into temp folder with specifically minimal permissions? The workaround could be to not use system temp folder, but create a temp folder in conda pkgs directory so that permissions are retained. ## Environment Information
`conda info`

``` C:\Users\labdsvm-admin>conda info active environment : None user config file : C:\Users\labdsvm-admin\.condarc populated config files : conda version : 4.5.5 conda-build version : 3.10.9 python version : 3.6.4.final.0 base environment : C:\Anaconda (writable) channel URLs : https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/main/win-64 https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/main/noarch https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/free/win-64 https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/free/noarch https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/r/win-64 https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/r/noarch https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/pro/win-64 https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/pro/noarch https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/msys2/win-64 https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/msys2/noarch package cache : C:\Anaconda\pkgs C:\Users\labdsvm-admin\AppData\Local\conda\conda\pkgs envs directories : C:\Anaconda\envs C:\Users\labdsvm-admin\AppData\Local\conda\conda\envs C:\Users\labdsvm-admin\.conda\envs platform : win-64 user-agent : conda/4.5.5 requests/2.18.4 CPython/3.6.4 Windows/10 Windows/10.0.14393 administrator : True netrc file : None offline mode : False ```

issue_title: Enhance update --all with new solver
Not obvious how to incorporate custom channels into conda recipe
issue_body:
I am creating a package where I know that the only place to find some of the required dependency packages come from certain not-obvious channels.  How do I specify that in the `meta.yaml` file?

I would like to be able to do:

```json
requirements:
  build:
    - cyclus/java-jre
```

or failing that to do:

```json
channels:
  - cyclus

requirements:
  build:
    - java-jre
```
I can’t see any way to do this in `meta.yaml` and I can't find any guidance on how to address this issue in any of the conda documentation.  This seems like such an obvious use case.  People who build packages will often have them be dependent on other "non-standard" packages.  There needs to be a package-creator mechanism for encoding the channels those "non-standard" packages come from.

I appreciate that the end-user *may* want to grab dependencies from some different set of channels: we should certainly support that.  But the default (if the end-user installing the package hasn't said anything different) surely must be to take whatever packages from whatever channels the package creator specifies.

Having to include external documentation (to make it a manual process) saying “and by the way to build or install this package you need to add `-c foo -c bar` or it absolutely won’t work” seems like the wrong answer.

issue_title: conda needs to be more forgiving of bad channel responses
issue_body:
While investigating the cause of #1036 I looked into the `.condarc` file which specifies:

```
channels:
  - https://conda.anaconda.org/t/an-c89909f5-b1ad-4c73-9500-9e5f0e710c33/anaconda-nb-extensions
  - http://localhost:8080/conda/anaconda
  - defaults
```

The second entry is for a local deployment of Anaconda Repository.  It turns out the license had expired, so the responses conda got from that channel were bogus.  That is mentioned in more than one place, but at least one of them is https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1189.

Because Anaconda Repository did not return the JSON result that conda was expecting, conda then threw this stack trace:

```

issue_title: headers assumed to be ascii encoded
issue_body:
So, with python3 pysam attempts to decode all headers as 'ascii' encoded strings which is not necessarily true:

`
>>> import pysam
>>> pysam.__version__
'0.14.1'
>>> locale.getpreferredencoding(False)
'UTF-8'
>>> cram = pysam.AlignmentFile("data.dir/21653_3#270.cram", "rc")
>>> cram.header
<pysam.libcalignmentfile.AlignmentHeader object at 0x7f62e0793ab0>
>>> print(cram.header)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "pysam/libcalignmentfile.pyx", line 530, in pysam.libcalignmentfile.AlignmentHeader.__str__
  File "pysam/libcutils.pyx", line 90, in pysam.libcutils.from_string_and_size
UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xe2 in position 548: ordinal not in range(128)
`

and see libcutils.pyx lines 88-92:
`
cdef from_string_and_size(const char* s, size_t length):
    if IS_PYTHON3:
        return s[:length].decode("ascii")
    else:
        return s[:length]
`

Not sure how best this should be fixed? Perhaps pysam.AlignmentFile could accept an "encoding" argument?

issue_title: bedtool.tabix_intervals: Too many open files error
issue_body:
Hi,
I'm using `metaseq` (https://github.com/daler/metaseq)  and it fails with **Too many open files** error while parallel process large *.bed files. This issue is due to `bedtool.tabix_intervals()` method:

1. `metaseq` splits input in lots of chunks for parallel processing
2. each chunk is processed with `metaseq.filetype_adapters.__getitem__()` which gets intervals iterator using pybedtools
3. `bedtool.tabix_intervals()` opens `tbx` file, but never close it

I see two possible fixes:
1) Close tbx file in `tabix_intervals()`
```
def tabix_intervals(self, interval_or_string):
        #...
        tbx = pysam.TabixFile(self.fn)
        results = list(tbx.fetch(str(interval.chrom), interval.start, interval.stop))
        tbx.close()
        return BedTool(results)
```
But in this case BedTool will create intermediate tmp file for results (see #73) and `metaseq` should do not forget to clean up loads of such tmp files

2) The other way is return bedtool object and tbx file descriptor pair and let user to close tbx file after handling bedtool
```
def tabix_intervals(self, interval_or_string):
        #...

       return (BedTool(results), tbx)
```
It breaks code compatibility but doesn't create intermediate tmp files. For my own purposes I've quick fixed `pybedtools` and `metaseq` using this way

I think my both suggested ways have disadvantages and some other way should be found.

issue_title: [FEATURE] Add ability to filter/rollover aliases using regex (similar to filtertype: pattern)
issue_body:
In our cluster we have around 10-20 indexes which use rollover feature and orchestrated by curator.
We also use aliases to tag inactive(rolled) indexes.
To target inactive indexes I use "action: alias"  and select all indexes except active write index.
Without ability to use regexp to filter aliases I have to edit curator action file every time new index added, only to add new index name.
If I could use filter to select aliases with prefix based pattern I'd be able to use one action file to manage all indexes. And all new indexes that follow naming convention (start with prefix) would be automatically picked up by curator.

Maybe it's possible:
- extend 'filtertype: pattern' to support aliases as well (just use parameter to enable "alias mode"), then we could use pattern instead of 'filtertype: alias'
- extend 'action: rollover' to support filters - so filter would return list of alias names where rollover has to be applied

Example of action file:
 ```
 1:
    action: rollover
    description: >-
      Rollover all active indexes where alias matches regexp(prefix) 'write_*'
    options:
      # select active index (all aliases that begin with "write_")
      # use prefix/regex instead of full name 
      name: write_
      conditions:
        max_docs: 40000000
  2:
    action: alias
    description: >-
      Set alias 'rolled' on rolled inactive indexes
    options:
      ignore_empty_list: True
      continue_if_exception: False
      disable_action: False
      name: rolled
    add:
      filters:
      - filtertype: pattern
        kind: prefix
        value: myindex-
      - filtertype: alias
        # exclude active write index , all aliases that begin with "write_"
        # use prefix/regex instead of full name
        aliases: write_
        exclude: True

```

issue_title: Incorrect exception handling in Transport
issue_body:
Hi!

My case:

```py
client = Elasticsearch('melk-vla-01.test.net', port=9202,  timeout=15, max_retries=1, retry_on_timeout=False)
s = Search(using=client, index='my_index').filter(..)
response = s.execute()
```

Port 9202 is closed by firewall, so `ConnectionTimeout` exception is expected here.

And there is what `connection.perform_request` returns: 

```
ConnectionError((<urllib3.connection.HTTPConnection object at 0x1089de1d0>, u'Connection to melk-vla-01.test.net timed out. (connect timeout=15)')) caused by: ConnectTimeoutError((<urllib3.connection.HTTPConnection object at 0x1089de1d0>, u'Connection to melk-vla-01.test.net timed out. (connect timeout=15)'))
```

But thats `ConnectionError` that **caused** by `ConnectTimeoutError`, so option `retry_on_timeout` dont worked here :(

Maybe its a good reason to add new option `retry_on_connect_error`? Or inspect reason of `ConnectionError` and find that problem in `ConnectTimeoutError`?

issue_title: vm extension set: autoUpgradeMinorVersion by default
issue_body:
I haven't even checked if the CLI has "az vm extension set" command (I'm mobile right now). 

But if there is any, we should make sure when we set extensions we should set autoUpgradeMinorVersion:true by default (and give users a knob to disable it). 

(This is basically the version:1.* feature in ASM)

This is good and PowerShell cmdlets do this too. 

1. It helps our users get highest minor version. 
2. It helps us reduce fragmentation on by offering users to install highest minor version available as they might be reading some old doc.

issue_title: [Network] NIC create/update should support custom DNS servers
issue_body: NIC creation currently supports only `--internal-dns-name`. However, there are other DNS properties that can be set via the portal. The CLI should support these scenarios as well.

issue_title: Issues with 'az network dns zone update'
issue_body:
Issues with 'az network dns zone update':

-h help text has two groups of parameters both under the same heading 'Generic Update Arguments'.  These should be grouped into a single group, containing add/remove/set
-not clear how add/remove/set should be used.  For example, how do I add, edit or remove ARM Tags using this command?  Needs concrete examples.
-Supporting If-none-match is pointless, it's only useful when creating zones, and that should be done via 'az network dns zone create'.  We should support the option to control create-if-not-exist there, not here.

issue_title: `az storage blob list` should clarify the meaning of `--prefix`
issue_body:
The description of `--prefix` is pretty straightforward, but I think
that it would be better if there was an additional warning there saying
that it is just a plain *literal prefix*.  In other words, it should
clarify that a listing with `--prefix foo` would show all of `foo`,
`foo2`, and `foo/bar` (when they exist, of course).

This would be helpful in avoiding an illusion that this is a similar
operation to getting a list of files in a directory.  (Something that I
think is impossible with azure storage due to its flat namespace
design.)

issue_title: azure.storage.blob.baseblobservice. BaseBlobService .exists logs error if blob does not exist

### Which service(blob, file, queue) does this issue concern?
`azure.storage.blob.baseblobservice.BaseBlobService.exists` 

### What problem was encountered?
When checking if a blob exists in container with `azure.storage.blob. baseblobservice.exists`, an error is logged when the blob does not exist in container. The expected behavior would be that no error is logged when the blob does not exist. 

### Have you found a mitigation/solution?
Hint:
`azure.storage.blob.baseblobservice.BaseBlobService.get_blob_properties` sends a request via `_perform_request` that raises an exception which is logged due to https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/blob/master/azure-storage-common/azure/storage/common/storageclient.py#L348

issue_title: Cython and Python versions of CIMultiDict treat key case differently
issue_body:
First noticed this in 3.3.2, still exists in 4.0.0:

As one would expect (IMO) the Cython version uses the title-ized key for matching and preserves the original key for output/serialization. The Python version throws away the original key completely and uses the title-ized key for both purposes, breaking output. Again, that's my opinion, but in any case, the behavior should be the same.

```python
In [1]: from multidict import CIMultiDict

In [2]: md = CIMultiDict()
   ...: md['some_key'] = 1
   ...: list(md.items())
   ...:
Out[2]: [('some_key', 1)]  # outputs original key

In [3]: from multidict._multidict_py import CIMultiDict

In [4]: md = CIMultiDict()
   ...: md['some_key'] = 1
   ...: list(md.items())
   ...:
Out[4]: [('Some_Key', 1)]  # outputs title-ized key
```

issue_title: Yum broken when RPM from this requirement with underscores.
issue_body:
Please never use underscores in python-packages names.

[PEP-008 conatins](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#package-and-module-names)
> Python packages should also have short, all-lowercase names, although the use of underscores is discouraged.

We have a really problem. When our build system convert all dependencies to RPM (using [FPM](https://github.com/jordansissel/fpm)) yum is broken. We are can't use this package in our projects.

issue_title: Nbgrader as a webapp
issue_body:
Much of the nbgrader complexity for the user could be reduced if it were a webapp for jupyterhub. It could:

* manage the enrolled students (add, delete, edit students)
* manage the assignments (create, edit, release, preview, grading, return)

Currently, items are spread out in nbgrader_config.py, the sqlite database, and in the file system (local source, release, autograded, and exchange versions). Initially, there doesn't need to be too much javascript, but just coding as a tornado webapp, getting its auth from jupyterhub.

One could also still allow programming uses (say, having programmatic access to the gradebook), but the webapp could allow 80% or more of what instructors do.

issue_title: movement of the notebook upon evaluation
issue_body:
I'm using SageMath 8.1 on Windows 10 Native with Jupyter Notebook.

When executing "Run All" on a SageMath notebook with many code cells (let say 100), the notebook moves in a very strange way: First it starts from the end of the notebook, and then it moves upward. 

When the top down "wave" of evaluation meets the bottom up movement of the notebook it stops moving. I would like the notebook to always show me the cell that is evaluated right now and move accordingly, i.e. downward until it gets to the end of the notebook.

As it works today, I have to search where is the current cell that is been evaluated and move the notebook manually to monitor progress. Moreover, there is no indication that the notebook is evaluation anything. There should be a flashing button that should indicate that the notebook is evaluation something and is not idle.

Thanks,

Daniel Volinski

issue_title: Where should we be storing frontend config?
issue_body:
- I thought we had agreed frontend config would go in platform specific directories (because users won't normally edit it directly), e.g. `~/.config/jupyter/nbconfig/` on Linux.
- Migration puts it in `~/.jupyter/nbconfig/`
- The notebook server looks for the files just in `~/.jupyter/`

We definitely need to ensure that the migration tool and the notebook server use the same folder for it. I think it's the notebook server that's wrong, because those files should have their own folder namespace.

issue_title: Optional function arguments
issue_body:
I'm not sure if this is an ipython bug or unique to jupyter notebooks. When a function I've defined -- and imported (defined in a .py file) -- has an optional parameter that would be passed by reference, and a variable in the notebook has the same name, the default value is not used.

As an example, let's say I've defined the function f as,

    def f(s=[]):
        s.append(2)
        return s

and I have defined the variable `s=[4, 3]`. If I call the function `f()`, I expect it to return `[2]`, but it instead returns `[4, 3, 2]`.

This does not always happen, but I've seen it happen enough that I not have to pass all optional parameters for every call to my own functions, or else I risk running into this problem.

issue_title: Paste menu items do not trigger paste actions
issue_body:
I'm building an extension that listens for actions in the notebook so I track how notebooks are used and evolve over time.

One of the actions I listen for is `paste-cell-below`. This action is triggered when the user hits the paste button in the toolbar, but not when they select the "Paste Cell Below" item in the Edit menu. I believe this is due to the `enable_paste` function on [line 1566 of Notebook.js](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/f5f2eadd923f689336345ecacfdfd2592685a8e0/notebook/static/notebook/js/notebook.js#L1566) which links the menu items directly to functions. I think this may need to set each of the onclicks to call the relevant action (which will in turn call the function) rather than call the paste functions directly.

issue_title: to_native gets run multiple times when being validated
Ability to preserve original field ordering when overriding existing fields
issue_body:
When `Options.export_order=True`, fields created on sub-classes come after those of parent classes.  This makes sense most of the time, except when overriding an existing field, such as to change the default or list of choices.

For example:

```python
class Thing(Model):
    class Options:
        export_order = True

    name = StringType(default='foo')
    value = IntType()


class SubThing(Thing):
    name = StringType(default='bar')
    new = StringType()
```

The field order for `Thing` is `['name', 'value']`, but `SubThing` is `['value', 'name', 'new']`.   I think it should be the default behavior that the order for `SubThing` is `['name', 'value', 'new']`.  At the very least, there should be an attribute on `Options` to control this.

issue_title: Ephemeral volumes aren't raided in all case
issue_body:
If you use an instance type with two ephemeral drives, cgcloud will install mdadm during `create -IT` and then take the image. So mdadm is present in the image and will not need to be installed again when running `recreate` from the image. But if you use an instance type with 0 or 1 ephemeral drives for `create -IT`, mdadm will not be needed at that time and so must be installed during `recreate`. If, between `create` and `recreate` an update to the mdadm package is published, apt will fail to install mdadm during `recreate` because the on-disk package index is stale and refers to an non-existant .deb.

The solution is to install mdadm unconditionally during the regular setup. There still is a chance that the early installation of mdadm at boot time will fail during recreate if the package index on the base image is stale, too.

mesosbox@ip-172-31-18-187:~$ docker run --net=host -it ubuntu
root@ip-172-31-18-187:/# apt-get install curl
Reading package lists... Done
<snip>
Setting up curl (7.35.0-1ubuntu2) ...
Processing triggers for libc-bin (2.19-0ubuntu6.7) ...
Processing triggers for ca-certificates (20130906ubuntu2) ...
Updating certificates in /etc/ssl/certs... 164 added, 0 removed; done.
Running hooks in /etc/ca-certificates/update.d....done.
root@ip-172-31-18-187:/# curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
hannes_cgcloud--agent-box--mesos-box-support--toil-box
root@ip-172-31-18-187:/# curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/hannes_cgcloud--agent-box--mesos-box-support--toil-box
{
  "Code" : "Success",
  "LastUpdated" : "2016-02-18T02:12:05Z",
  "Type" : "AWS-HMAC",
  "AccessKeyId" : "ASIAJFDO76S5RV5XIXVA",
  "SecretAccessKey" : "<snip>",
  "Token" : "<snip>",
  "Expiration" : "2016-02-18T08:44:37Z"
}
root@ip-172-31-18-187:/#
```

>This means that you can simply strip all of the credentials-passing code and it will just work, with one caveat: the metadata service has a built-in request limit and boto doesn't do retries for it, so if too many processes on an instance request temporary credentials at the same time, it may fail. This typically happens in s3am on an instance with many cores since s3am schedules two processes per core by default and each process get the credentials separately. But this is on my roadmap. See

>https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/s3am/issues/23
> https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/toil/issues/688

issue_title: Add a parser for rfc2579 datetime format
issue_body:
https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2579

These timestamps are used in ipp cache files (https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2911).

```
DateAndTime ::= TEXTUAL-CONVENTION
    DISPLAY-HINT "2d-1d-1d,1d:1d:1d.1d,1a1d:1d"
    STATUS       current
    DESCRIPTION
            "A date-time specification.

            field  octets  contents                  range
            -----  ------  --------                  -----
              1      1-2   year*                     0..65536
              2       3    month                     1..12
              3       4    day                       1..31
              4       5    hour                      0..23
              5       6    minutes                   0..59
              6       7    seconds                   0..60
                           (use 60 for leap-second)
              7       8    deci-seconds              0..9
              8       9    direction from UTC        '+' / '-'
              9      10    hours from UTC*           0..13
             10      11    minutes from UTC          0..59

            * Notes:
            - the value of year is in network-byte order
            - daylight saving time in New Zealand is +13

            For example, Tuesday May 26, 1992 at 1:30:15 PM EDT would be
            displayed as:

                             1992-5-26,13:30:15.0,-4:0

```

issue_title: Question: Automatic Patch Updates for .NET Core
issue_body:
# Automatic Patch Updates for .NET Core

This isn't really an issue so much as a question. Our group here is attempting to start moving to .NET Core for our web applications. However, IT has some concerns about patching and automatic updates to .NET Core over time. The nice thing about the .NET Framework is that new versions are installed via Windows Updates, and for them, we only have to schedule maintenance windows.

We have hundreds of servers, and the concern is that moving to .NET Core will cause a serious bottleneck on resources every time an update is required.

Are there any known mechanisms we can use with Windows 10 / Server 2016+ to get newer versions of .NET Core without human intervention. Even if it means a powershell script during maintenance windows would be better than nothing. However, I'm not sure how we would even accomplish that.

Any thoughts?

issue_title: Well Known/Stable Links should be provided for downloading released versions of all tools.
issue_body:
# Issue
Currently, the tools are hidden by a `fwlink`, such as https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?linkid=841683, which represents the Window x64 tools for RC4.

Even after resolving the `fwlink` the resolved URL is not human parseable and is not consistent between releases. The above `fwlink` resolve to `https://download.microsoft.com/download/B/4/6/B4678511-01F4-4F97-902B-0E58A985932A/dotnet-dev-win-x64.1.0.0-rc4-004771.zip`, for example.

# Proposal
The tools should instead be provided at a well known stable address so they can be downloaded dynamically by build tools that are dependent on a specific version.

# Example
For example, in order to take a dependency on a specific version of NuGet, one can define a build variable such as:
`$NUGET_VERSION = "3.5.0"`

It can then be accessed from a well known URL:
`https://dist.nuget.org/win-x86-commandline/v$NUGET_VERSION/NuGet.exe`

If I then decide to take a dependency on a new version of NuGet, I merely need to change the definition of `$NUGET_VERSION`. For example, to move forward to `4.0.0-rc4`, I would change this to be `$NUGET_VERSION = "4.0.0-rc4"`.

In a similar manner, the dotnet/core tools should all be provided at a stable/well-known address, such as: `https://downloads.dot.net/dotnet-dev/v1.0.0-rc4-004771/win-x64.zip` and `https://downloads.dot.net/dotnet-dev/v1.0.0-rc4-004771/ubuntu-x64.zip`.

This allows users to write manageable (and readable) build scripts that are easily configurable to download specific versions of the dotnet tools for use in their build systems.

If URLs were provided in the above manner, then changing from rc3 to rc4 is as simple as changing the target version from `1.0.0-rc3-004530` to `1.0.0-rc4-004771`.

Here's the dependencies of the package:

I managed to workaround the issue by adding the following package references into csproj:
```
    <PackageReference Include="System.IO.FileSystem.Primitives" Version="4.3.0" />
    <PackageReference Include="System.Net.NameResolution" Version="4.3.0" />

```

If I build and run it for net462: 
```

So for net462 I added:
```

    <PackageReference Include="System.Security.Principal.Windows" Version="4.3.0" />
  </ItemGroup>
```
After that it builds and runs on net462 too.

But I think it's too cumbersome. Also it requires manual csproj editing and does not work via VS Package Manager out of the box. So I believe it's not a normal situation.

issue_title: Package name / versioning for CentOS packages
issue_body:
The package names for RPM distributions seem to be a bit whack. As things stand, you can't do point release upgrades on the system without manual intervention - which seems to be somewhat limiting.

For example, the latest runtime package is: dotnet-runtime-2.0.5-2.0.5-1.x86_64

This breaks down to:
* package name = dotnet-runtime-2.0.5
* version = 2.0.5
* revision = 1
* arch = x86_64

This means when you release 2.0.6 for example, it will be a manual upgrade to 2.0.6 as the actual package name will be dotnet-runtime-2.0.6 with version 2.0.6

Unless there is some abuse going on with the Obsoletes / Provides tags in the .spec file used to generate the RPM, I can't see how this fits in with proper practice for packaging on *nix based systems.

Generally, the package name would be set as 'dotnet-runtime', with a *version* of 2.0.5 - or even if it is a dotnet limitation, have 'dotnet-runtime-2.0' - which can then have a version of 2.0.5, 2.0.6, 2.0.7 etc etc delivered seamlessly as a package upgrade via the distro package manager.

issue_title: Client should be able to accept CSR's in either PEM or DER format.
issue_body:
This can be remedied most easily by using openssl to convert PEM CSR's into der. No private key is needed for that operation. the openssl shell code to perform the operation is below
```bash
openssl req -in pemcsr.csr  -inform PEM -out dercsr.der -outform DER
```

issue_title: Fastavro fallback doesn't prevent errors
issue_body:
It looks like the intent in `message_serializer.py` is to use `fastavro` and then to fallback to `avro.io` if it is not available.

Because `fastavro` doesn't seem to be able to fully replace `avro.io` and can fail deserialising messaged that are otherwise deserialised fine with `avro.io` the fallback should be more reliable.

**Currently**:
if `fastavro` is present, create a function using it and register as a deserialiser, otherwise register a function that uses `avro.io`

**Ideally**:
Give users an option on whether to use `fastavro` or not by not including it in dependencies in `setup.py` so `pip` does not automatically install one

**Acceptable**:
Register a decode function that tries `fastavro` first, and if it fails fallbacks to `avro.io` for every message.

issue_title: A docker compose example that works
issue_body:
Guys, I'm in the process of creating a k8s deployment of pypiserver. I just wanted to fix the docker-compose.yml so it's updated to the v3.3 format.

```yaml
---
version: "3.3"

services:
    pypiserver:
        image: pypiserver/pypiserver:latest
        volumes:
            - type: bind
              source: ./data
              target: /data
        command: -P /data/.htaccess -a update,download,list /data/packages
        ports:
            - "80:8080"
volumes:
    data:
```

You just need to create "data" and put a `.htaccess` file there with: `touch data/.htaccess && htpasswd data/.htaccess <username>`.

issue_title: Multiple clients with same credentials
issue_body:
<!--
0. The library is Python 3.x, not Python 2.x.
1. If you're posting an issue, make sure it's a bug in the library, not in your code.
2. If you're posting a question, make sure you have read and tried enough things first.
3. Show as much information as possible, including your failed attempts, and the full console output (to include the whole traceback with line numbers).
4. Good looking issues are a lot more appealing. If you need help check out https://guides.github.com/features/mastering-markdown/.

You may also want to watch "How (not) to ask a technical question" over https://youtu.be/53zkBvL4ZB4
-->

Hello.

First of all, **thanks** for such a nice library.

I have a **question** about creating multiple instances of `client` with the **same** credentials.

### The story
Let's say, i have a `main.py`, i do all the required stuff to make it work and then start `idle`.

But one day, i wanted to send a message asynchronously using `Celery`. Since i **can't** pass `client` as an argument to the task, i have to **create** a **new** **instance** of `client` and send a message. And it **worked**! Message from the _Celery task_ was successfully sent. **But...** 

Remember `main.py` and its client that is in `idle` "mode"? It **does not see** any new updates. It is still `is_connected==True` and `is_user_authorized==True`, even more, it is still trying hard to work: 
```
DEBUG:telethon.telegram_bare_client:Receiving items from the network timed out
DEBUG:telethon.telegram_bare_client:Receiving items from the network...
DEBUG:telethon.extensions.tcp_client:socket.timeout "timed out" while reading data
DEBUG:telethon.telegram_bare_client:Receiving items from the network timed out
...
and so on
```

### Trying to fight
I was investigating this problem for awhile. 

**What i have tried**:
- to set `update_workers` to `1` in the`main.py`, in the `task` and in both
- to play with `spawn_read_thread`
- to manually disconnect client inside the `task` after the job was done

Nothing of listed above helped. :( 

### THE WIN!
But apparently, i have found something interesting in the _docs_ - `.sync_updates()`. Well, i decided to run this every time task was done. But how should i do this? `idle` locked my `main.py`, so i had to get rid of it. So, i wrote my own simple
```python
while True:
    sleep(1)
    client.sync_updates()
```

And it **worked**!

It seems like, when i called `connect` in another `client` "entity", it acquired update handling, so my `main.py` `client` couldn't  receive anything.

### Questions
 - Is this a proper way of handling this kind of problem or you can suggest me anything else?
 - Is `.sync_updates()` cheap or expensive? I am a little worried how calling this every 1 second will effect performance? 
- If it is a good way of handling such problems and it is not expensive, is it possible to add this thing to `idle`? :-)

Regards,
@Senpos

issue_title: InputPhoneContact should work without passing client_id
issue_body:
In TLSharp InputPhoneContact can be created without passing client_id. For example for creating a new contact like this example: 
https://stackoverflow.com/a/40850765/3769451

issue_title: HDF5 Performance Tuning
issue_body:
Split out from #19 to avoid that topic from losing too much focus.

So I have spent some time in the past trying to tune the HDF5 settings of loompy (see [here](https://github.com/linnarsson-lab/loom-viewer/issues/90) and [here](https://github.com/JobLeonard/loompy-chunk-cache-experiments/blob/master/Loompy%20cache%20test.ipynb)), looking for the best trade-off in terms of compression size and disk access speed.

The very weird result so far is that regardless of the overrides I have tried for the chunk-cache, loading times remain the same, even if I set it to zero. I am on a maching with 64GB of RAM, I should be able to blow up the chunk cache to crazy numbers, and given the 64x64 chunk size see 64x less disk access. But nothing happens.

I'm thinking of opening a topic on the H5PY Google Groups to help me figure out what is wrong here, because the numbers make no sense. I guess that at the moment everything uses the default 1MB chunk cache, despite overrides. This is _ridiculously_ low on modern machines, and far from adequate for larger data sets (assuming 64x64 chunks and 32bit floats, you cannot even load a single row without evicting part of the chunk cache if you have more than ~6000 cells).

Another issue to discuss is the row- or column- major layout. This would focus on the low-level implementation question, the more "high level" discussion of the convention should remain in #19. HDF5 is neither, but loading data from HDF5 files through h5py will result in row-major Numpy arrays.

All of these questions also apply to connecting/creating loom files through R.

issue_title: Independence optimization
issue_body:
## Description
Add independence-optimization for constraint solving. 
When a state with constraint set C needs to add a new constraint c’, instead of passing C ∧ c’ to the constraint solver to test satisfiability, pass R(C, c’) ∧ c’ where the expression R(C, c’)  returns only the constraints in C that have symbolic variables also in c’.

### Benefits
This optimization reduces the difficulty of the query and also increases constraint cache hits.

issue_title: Sacred crashes with an exception when working directory does not directly contain the main script.
issue_body:
I have a python script inside of a package that creates a run a sacred experiment using automain.  In simplified terms, my directory structure looks like this:

```
root
+--data
+--mypackage
      +--__init__.py
      +--myexperiment.py
```

myexperiment.py looks like this:

```
from sacred import Experiment

ex = Experiment("My Experiment")

@ex.automain
def run_main():
    ...
```

When I run this experiment, by running:

`python mypackage/myexperiment.py`

It crashes with an exception.  When I traced down the issue, it seems that the problem is that when it gathers the names of the source files used in the experiments, it registers the filename of the main script only `myexperiment.py`, nots its path.  It then later tries to read the script file using that file name, but since the file name is resolved relative to root, it fails to find the file and throws an exception.

Since I have a complex project and lots of experiments, its necessary to organize things into folders/packages, so keeping all the experiment scripts in the root folder would be highly inconvenient.

I'm not sure if it makes a difference, but I'm using the SQLObserver.

issue_title: Z3 produces "unknown" on simple problem
issue_body:
Z3 produces unknown on the following problem:

(declare-const I Int)
(declare-const J Int)
(declare-const S Int)
(declare-const SP Int)
(declare-const N Int)
(assert (<= 0 I)) 
(assert (<= 0 J))
(assert (= S (/ (\* J (+ J 1)) 2)))
(assert (= SP (/ (\* (+ (- N I) 1) (+ I N)) 2)))
(assert (= (+ I J) (+ N 1)))
(check-sat)

By commenting out _any_ of the assertions, Z3 is able to find a model.

For example, commenting out the first assertion produces the following model:

(model 
  (define-fun S () Int
    0)
  (define-fun J () Int
    0)
  (define-fun SP () Int
    0)
  (define-fun I () Int
    1)
  (define-fun N () Int
    0)
)

But note that the first assertion holds in this model. Furthermore, assertions 3 and 4 are superfluous, since S and SP do not appear anywhere else.

I understand of course that Z3 cannot be complete, but I suspect that this behaviour is indeed a bug.

issue_title: [FS] appendbytes should raise TypeError
issue_body:
Hi there,
while rewriting the documentation, I realised that `FS.appendbytes` raises `ValueError`, but shouldn't: according to doc of `ValueError` and `TypeError`, `ValueError` should be raised when given *Inappropriate argument value (of correct type)*, while `TypeError` should be raised when given *Inappropriate argument type*.

Since the exception is raised when `FS.appendbytes(path, data)` is called with `data` not being a `bytes` instance, the exception should definitely be a `TypeError`.

**NB**: same goes for `appendtext`, `setbytes` and `settext`.

issue_title: __float__ method makes numpy/pandas forget currency
issue_body:
```
>>> pandas.DataFrame([moneyed.Money()]).sum()
0    0.00 XYZ
dtype: object

>>> pandas.DataFrame([djmoney.money.Money()]).sum()
0    0.0  # <-- currency missing
dtype: float64
```

This is due to implementing the `__float__` method:

```py
class MM(moneyed.Money):
    def __float__(self):
        return float(self.amount)
```

```
>>> pandas.DataFrame([MM()]).sum()
0    0.0  # <-- currency missing
dtype: float64
```

I'm not saying the `__float__` method should be removed/changed because I'm not sure of what use it is. Just wanted to leave a note about this here.

issue_title: Dry run not working with transactions are slow
issue_body:
It appears that when running with transactions enabled, the semantics of dry_run changes. This means that in preview mode it will potentially start doing all kinds of crazy database stuff, only to rollback the transaction in case the user doesn't confirm the changes. For our app with a rather complicated save, this makes preview 10 times slower than it should be.

The following patch reverts so that dry_run can be used with transactions as well:

https://github.com/arj03/django-import-export/commit/528ca8fbfc3ad342b4365804a1e9e6a487b84c61

I don't see why we can't both have transactions and dry_run, they should be orthogonal.

This is what Pandas uses for S3 and given that this project already has a dependency on Pandas it will not add an "exotic" dependency. Not to say that tests should not be written but if we were to spend time on it we may as well refactor the code and use s3fs.

Matt:
> I generally agree with that approach. I'ved used https://github.com/jubos/fake-s3 in the past with a before hook launch -- but it adds ruby as a dependency to tests so I wouldn't recommend it here.
> 
> I haven't used s3fs before but your argument sounds solid. There may be a case to be made to add a minimal test here without too much refactor and then go a bigger PR with the swap-over. I'd leave that judgement call to you but I can probably help with s3fs changes/testing later on as well.

issue_title: Use parameter types to convert arguments
issue_body:
Right now all arguments passed as `-p foo 2` are created as `float`s in the notebook. When using parameters to specify shapes of numpy arrays and the like you end up having to convert them to integers first. We could use the type of the value of the parameter as a guide and convert arguments to that type.

So a "parameters" cell with `foo = 2.123` and `papermill ... -p foo 2` would result in `2.` but a cell with `foo = 2` would result in `2`.

issue_title: Add default null log handler
issue_body:
#### Code Sample, a minimal, complete, and verifiable piece of code

```python
>>> from satpy import Scene
>>> scn = Scene(...)
No handlers could be found for logger "satpy.readers.yaml_reader"
```
#### Problem description

Many users get confused by the above **warning** message. To be clear for any users who see this thinking it is an error, **this is just a warning**. Code should still work perfectly fine assuming you have the input files you think you have and they are supported by satpy.

#### Expected Output

No warning message about loggers so users don't confuse it with an error message.

#### Possible Solution

If I recall correctly we can add a NullHandler to the satpy logger: https://docs.python.org/2/howto/logging.html#configuring-logging-for-a-library

issue_title: Adding default CSRF header as a good security practice .
issue_body:
It is no harm to set X-Requested-By when csrf protection is disabled. This will help user experience so when livy CsrfFilter check for "X-Requested-By" header it doesn't return a ""Missing Required Header for CSRF protection."
Check the usage of CSRF headers at owasp.
The main idea is to check the presence of a custom header (agreed-upon between the server and a client – e.g. X-CSRF or X-Requested-By) in all state-changing requests coming from the client.

issue_title: Unexpected artifact in depth stream when capturing through transparent surfaces
issue_body:
| Required Info                         |                                                                |
|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------- |
| Camera Model                       | SR300 | 
| Firmware Version                   | _multiple_ | 
| Operating System & Version |   Win 8.1 / Linux | 
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)    |  4.4.0-101                                         | 
| Platform                                 | PC |
| SDK Version                            | 2.9.1                          | 


### Description

We have been using different setups (PC, embedded platforms, etc) and various OS (Linux, Win) to test this behaviour and it is quite consistent (we tested on various SR300 firmwares and scanners, too).

We are trying to scan through transparent material, and we see a artifact, always at the same spot, when doing so.

We have tested on 3 different types of PMMA (obviously, glass is a no go since it's opaque to IR), including one that is opaque to visible light. For each test, we capture the scene that is after the material quite well (proportions, dimensions are respected and the model is as clean as if captured without the transparent material) BUT for a little spot in a specific place in the stream.
The PMMAs are clean and there is no default on the surface that could explain the artifact.

This "spot" is linked to the orientation of the transparent material : at orientations roughly > 45°, it gets out of the frustum and is not detectable anymore. But when the material is somewhat parallel to the lens, it's here.

We tested in a dark room too, to eliminate any visible light incidence.

I have attached pictures for 3 tests that we have done
(1) Transparent PMMA

![capture1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1944007/39802230-cf958a4c-536d-11e8-93b4-5cf2f10077a8.JPG)
![setup1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1944007/39802231-cfae0b44-536d-11e8-8f34-c64c4903cf94.jpg)

(2) Opaque-to-Visible-Light PMMA

![capture2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1944007/39802227-cf4e16f8-536d-11e8-9b2b-e771f553aa35.JPG)
![setup2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1944007/39802228-cf66ee9e-536d-11e8-8aba-c997c985e499.jpg)

(3) Different Transparent PMMA

![capture3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1944007/39802226-cf36eb0e-536d-11e8-8fed-54414ea0c528.JPG)
![setup3](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1944007/39802229-cf7d892e-536d-11e8-92fd-1249d5acb532.jpg)

(1bis) Transparent PMMA at an angle (spot is no more visible)

![capture1atangle](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1944007/39802224-cf1ed3e8-536d-11e8-89c0-23cf7ce51f76.JPG)


My question is: 
  - Do you guys have any idea why this happens ? It looks kind of a reflection of IR, but why would it only be a single spot ? The index of refraction differs between the two surfaces obviously but I don't see how the law of refraction would give such a result
  - Could this be a limitation of the library in itself ?
  - Can it be detected / removed by the lib ? We have tried to detect it and remove it via reconstruction algorithms (poisson, marching cubes etc) with smoothing parameters but it's not up to what we expect

Any suggestion welcome ;)
Thanks a lot
Best

issue_title: Unity: Changing image size in RealsenseStreamTexture.ProcessFrame
issue_body:
I'm subclassing RealsenseStreamTexture and overriding ProcessFrame to pass it through a bunch of filters. If one of those filters changes the image size (a decimate filter, for example), I'd like to resize the texture as well, but there doesn't seem to be an elegant way to do this.

I am able to access the texture member because it is marked protected, but I can't do much with it in ProcessFrame because ProcessFrame might not be called in the main unity thread. Calling texture.width, for example, results in the error "GetDataWidth can only be called from the main thread".

I could try changing the texture in Update, but  RealsenseStreamTexture.Update is private so I can't call it, and the code in there uses private members, so I can't recreate it.

Instead, in ProcessFrame I store the frame width and height and then use LateUpdate to check if the texture needs to be recreated. This seems to work but is hackish. (I am having problems with Unity not recognizing the change until I deactivate and reactivate any components that use the changed shader, but this seems to be a Unity problem).

It would be nice if RealsenseStreamTexture checked for size changes after calling ProcessFrame and resized the texture appropriately.

Cheers

issue_title: Distributed training discrepancy from original PPO paper
issue_body: The original PPO paper describes aggregating data from multiple workers and optimizing the surrogate loss with minibatch size <= NT, where N is the number of workers and T is the number of timesteps per worker. However, in the baselines implementation, it seems that gradients are calculated on a worker-by-worker basis, and then averaged. One negative consequence of this, as far as I can see, is that the minibatch size must be <= T, which might be problematic in distributed settings where the number of workers is large and so, for data efficiency, T is small. Is this an intentional change from the original paper?

issue_title: Subclass InputObjectType like test_425
issue_body:
There is a bug [here](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/3ce6031192c7be1b7986448f18a227b1f971b6a3/graphene/types/inputobjecttype.py#L57) which is quite similar to [test_425](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/2.0/graphene/tests/issues/test_425.py) which prevents creating a custom `Meta` for `InputObjectType`.

The fix to this is to replicate what is done with [`ObjectType.__init_subclass_with_meta__`](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/blob/3ce6031192c7be1b7986448f18a227b1f971b6a3/graphene/types/objecttype.py#L31) -- i.e. don't re-create `_meta` if it's already supplied.

I'll try and find another workaround, but as @syrusakbary seems to be busy with other things (issues & pull requests don't seem to be readily addressed) I'm not convinced putting in the work to make a pull request is worth it.

issue_title: Remove monkey-patched LoginView methods in apps.py
issue_body:
In apps.py, two methods of LoginView have been monkey-patched with the decorated versions from django-axes. This makes the library methods unusable as they have been replaced with their decorated versions. This is a serious problem in django applications that do not use the vanilla django LoginView and create other LoginViews by inheriting from django LoginView.
Also, now django-axes can no longer be used with django-simple-captcha since a new LoginView with CaptchaFields cannot be created.

We had to create workarounds for this problem (https://github.com/freedombox/Plinth/pull/1155/files#diff-731aef8cc92c441d1d49eed734cb4f46) to be able to use django-simple-captcha.

This monkey-patching makes a huge assumption that users of this library never create inherited LoginViews. The documentation should be updated to let the users know how to decorate their LoginViews themselves.

issue_title: Conflict with django_jinja
issue_body:
We upgraded from django-debug-toolbar 1.9 to 1.10 and ended up with a error that seems to break compatibility with django_jinja.

We received the same error as this Stack Overflow question:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/38569760/django-debug-toolbar-template-object-has-no-attribute-engine

Line 175 in `panels.templates.panel.py` raises:
```
Exception Value: 'Template' object has no attribute 'engine'
```

This seems to have it's origin in the way that template dirs are grabbed, by pulling the engine info from an individual template:
```
        # Fetch context_processors/template_dirs from any template
        if self.templates:
            context_processors = self.templates[0]['context_processors']
            template_dirs = self.templates[0]['template'].engine.dirs
        else:
            context_processors = None
            template_dirs = []
```

You can see in this commit, that debug_toolbar used to use a `get_template_dirs()` method that used `django.template.engine.Engine.get_default()` and `engine.dirs` to pull in the template dirs. I haven't tested this, but I suspect this was the breaking change.
https://github.com/jazzband/django-debug-toolbar/commit/6390279a5eba1dc47fc12627204484fda67d3825#diff-f6bb86ee9199645f22f9b23d002ed707L24

This seems to work:

```python
from django.template.engine import Engine
engine = Engine.get_default()
engine.dirs 
```

We're encountered this with:
```
Django==1.10 (also tried with 1.9.9)
django_jinja==2.2.0
Jinja2==2.8
django-debug-toolbar==1.5
```

issue_title: Create an otPlat API to handle vendor / platform specific Spinel properties.
issue_body:
Occasions exist where a host needs to send commands that are specific to its platform. Such commands should not be implemented by Openthread but should be handled by code that is technically separate from Openthread but still part of the NCP image.  Because Spinel and ncp_base get first opportunity to parse incoming frames from the host, it becomes necessary for ncp_base to have a way to route vendor specific requests to an appropriate handler.

For this reason it is proposed to create a new otPlat API that ncp_base will call for any Spinel property frames that it cannot handle internally.  Calls to this API shall be configured at build time via a core config variable that shall be disabled by default.

issue_title: Client.publishEvent releases _messagesLock even if not acquired
issue_body:
In `ibmiotf/application.py:377`, `_messagesLock` is acquired only if publishing was successful (`if result[0] == paho.MQTT_ERR_SUCCESS`), but it is released irrespectively in the `final:` block. This results in:

```
  File "C:\AgentWork\c2889035b7ac6605\.testenv\lib\site-packages\ibmiotf\device.py", line 307, in publishEvent
    self._messagesLock.release()
RuntimeError: release unlocked lock
```

This can happen if the client is disconnected when a message is sent (in which case paho will return `MQTT_ERR_NO_CONN`).

* This seems to have been broken accidentally as a part of https://github.com/ibm-watson-iot/iot-python/commit/ff473cead15ab9a26aa3baaea2efe1f9c8342a3c
* A simple way to reproduce this is to manually call `.disconnect()`, then call `publishEvent`.

I'm not 100% sure which code `_messagesLock` should protect; I _think_ the try-finally can be moved into the `if` statement, i.e.,

```python
                if result[0] == paho.MQTT_ERR_SUCCESS:
                    try:
                        self._messagesLock.acquire()
                        if result[1] in self._onPublishCallbacks:
                            # paho callback beat this thread so call callback inline now
                            del self._onPublishCallbacks[result[1]]
                            if on_publish is not None:
                                on_publish()
                        else:
                            # this thread beat paho callback so set up for call later
                            self._onPublishCallbacks[result[1]] = on_publish
                    finally:
                        self._messagesLock.release()
                    return True
```

issue_title: Rename repo, and coordinate with new release.
Problem installing python driver
issue_body:
* bigchaindb-driver version: -
* bigchaindb **server** version (`bigchaindb -v`): 1.01
* Python version: 3.6.4
* Operating System: Linux - Ubuntu 17.10

### Description

Hi all! I’m currently developing an IoT project as my End of Degree in Industrial Engineering Project, and the goal is to implement it with Blockchain technology to have it completely decentralized. I’m quite noob both in programming and with blockchain technologies and I am a bit lost so any help will be greatly appreciated!

Up to now, the project (programmed in python) it can be seen in the following repo if someone wants to take a look at it

https://github.com/rromanss23/Uploading-sensor-data-to-IOTA-tangle

And works as follows:

    There’s environment data collected from a sensor attached to a Raspberry Pi.
    Data is published via MQTT.
    A linux machine is subscribed to the MQTT channel and:

issue_title: distutils compatibility is broken (and pointless)
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **Michał Górny (Bitbucket: [mgorny](http://bitbucket.org/mgorny), GitHub: [mgorny](http://github.com/mgorny))**

----------------------------------------

The code reads:

    try:
        from setuptools import setup, find_packages
    except ImportError:
        from distutils.core import setup
        def find_packages():
            return ['sqlpython']
                    ^^^^^^^^^^^

which means that anyone trying to install it without setuptools is going to get wrong package name. Aside of that, setuptools-specific keys will trigger warnings in Python 2, lack of 2to3 conversion in Python 3 and fatal errors in Python 3.3.

I suggest just importing `setuptools` and forgetting about distutils compatibility.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/catherinedevlin/cmd2/issue/7

issue_title: How should people install 3rd party plugins outside of certbot-auto?
issue_body:
Currently the way Certbot plugins work is we use [setuptools entry_points](https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#dynamic-discovery-of-services-and-plugins). This means the plugin must be installed at a path in [sys.path](https://docs.python.org/2/library/sys.html#sys.path).

For this reason, most of our 3rd party plugins tell people to run `(sudo) pip install` outside of a virtual environment to get their plugin. This isn't great as it can cause conflicts with packages installed from the users OS. Telling 3rd party plugins they need to get packaged by the distro also isn't a great option.

How can we reduce headaches here? Could Certbot have a canonical place for a virtual environment that Certbot also searches if its permissions are sane?

For context, the reason this is a bigger issue now is you need a DNS plugin for each provider so lots of new plugins are getting written and we don't want to take on the maintenance burden of all of them.

This requires a little research into best practices here, but we should start reporting that our ACME module is dropping Python 3.3 support using best practices for a library and we should start logging at the warning level in Certbot about the same problem.

cc @joohoi and @SwartzCr another good target

issue_title: Implement a "script" plugin and/or fix manual for scriptability
issue_body:
I guess I'm not the first one to raise the issue, but I haven't found anything yet by searching.

I'm trying to automate VM deployment with a letsencrypt certificate. So I have tried to remove user interaction as much as possible and reached:

```
sudo /opt/letsencrypt/letsencrypt-auto certonly --manual -m contact@example.com -d example.com -t --manual-public-ip-logging-ok --agree-tos
```

But if I add the `-n` option, I get:

> `PluginError('Running manual mode non-interactively is not supported',)`

I'm guessing the point is that manual mode _does_ require user interaction hence cannot be fully done in non-interactive mode. At least during the file-validation step.

Which is why I'd like to discuss splitting the command in two steps:

```
sudo /opt/letsencrypt/letsencrypt-auto certonly --manual -m contact@example.com -d example.com -t --manual-public-ip-logging-ok --agree-tos --generate-file /home/user
```

which would create the file `/home/user/.well-known/acme-challenge/buCgmexKDQypJfWE4-XmUfP1cpILzsBR4UWRE_o8iuc` with the expected content and then running

```
sudo /opt/letsencrypt/letsencrypt-auto certonly --manual -m contact@example.com -d example.com -t --manual-public-ip-logging-ok --agree-tos --validate
```

would finish the process.

Would this make sense? Did I miss something?

Another option would be to build allow a script to be executed between the file generation step and the verification step:

```
sudo /opt/letsencrypt/letsencrypt-auto certonly --manual -m contact@example.com -d example.com -t --manual-public-ip-logging-ok --agree-tos --deploy-verification ./deploy-verification.sh
```

issue_title: Provide a receipt for payments
issue_body:
#### Problem

Our current approach is to let the payer choose the secret instead of the payee, this means that a secret reveal is not tied to the cannot be used as a proof of payment, the payee may say "I never received the secret".

Shifting the task of secret generation from the payer to the payee is a sufficient way to produce the receipt (the secret itself is a prove that the payment happened), but we lose the ability to retry the payment with different routes under timeouts.

#### Proposed solution

We can add a different composed lock to Raiden, built from these parts:

- A payer retry hashlock, this hashlock may be regenerated at will by the payer, allowing him to try different routes as timeouts happen.
- A payee receipt hashlock, this hashlock encodes the receipt from the payee, initially a hash of random secret seems sufficient.
- A time lock, used for the lock expiration controlled by the payer.

To unlock the transfer both secrets are required. This ReceiptHashlock has additional protocol requirements:

- The payment must be initiated by the payee, the payee sends a payment request to the payer with the receipt hashlock
  - The payee doesn't need to choose the expiration time of the lock, as this is route dependent and the payee can discard the receipt secret without risk of losing tokens. Additionally the payee will need a method to decide when the secret should be discarded, I think it's sufficient to do health checks and use a configurable *local* expiration for the payee.
- The payer uses the receipt hashlock and it's own retry hashlock to create the mediated transfer, the retry hashlock may be regenerated as new routes are tried.
- Once the payee receives the MediatedTransfer a SecretRequest is sent to the payer, the receipt secret is *not* revealed yet.
- The payer reveal it's secret, the end result is that the payee will have more information than the payer, but it's the payee interest to receive the tokens, so this disparity is no problem (whereas the opposite is not true, the payee may not reveal the receipt secret first, as that says "the payment happened" and the payer may just not reveal the retry secret).
- The payee reveals the receipt secret once the retry secret is learned.
- The secrets propagates from back to front as usual.

issue_title: JSONRPCClient's nonce got out of sync and spammed logs with nonce updates without actually updating them
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

On a testing session, after trying to do a deposit, which required an on-chain transaction, my Raiden node logs started spamming something like the following line in the stdout:
```
updated nonce from server          [raiden.network.rpc.client]  local=161 server=160
updated nonce from server          [raiden.network.rpc.client]  local=161 server=160
updated nonce from server          [raiden.network.rpc.client]  local=161 server=160
updated nonce from server          [raiden.network.rpc.client]  local=161 server=160
```
Maybe around 10-30x/sec.
The transaction didn't go through, nor the node code be interacted with anymore.
A kernel update required a reboot right after, and I couldn't reproduce the issue anymore (did 3 deposits and a channel-close after it).

Looking at the code, it became clear that the current nonce-update logic was not optimal, and a `cachetools.(TTLCache|cachedmethod)` could put it in a more consistent and reliable state. This led to the PR #1936 , which should fix this issue and possibly allow proper parallel-submitting of transactions.

The test probably occured with master on a2f5fda23b1ab61ecc20b5cca0169a19359c0f0a

issue_title: Unify `-q ` and `on-host`.
issue_body:
`sos run script -q queue`
`task: on_host='host'`

Two machines can be confusing (or is it?). host means another machine, most likely without queue, queue means a queuing system locally or remotely. Not sure if or how we can unify these two options (they would be implemented the same way #441).

Noticing that `-h` and `--host` are not possible from command line, perhaps we should rename `task: on_host='host'` to `task: queue='host'`. It is just a bit weird to use `queue` option to run a job that require `sos` to wait.

Of course we could implement task in a way that all tasks are executed separately and sos does not wait for them, so even in the remote execution case we can limit the number of concurrent execution job by `-j` (max jobs).

issue_title: Wrap “Grade or comment on multiple submissions” methods
issue_body:
The Canvas REST API offers [a suite of "Grade or comment on multiple submissions" methods](https://canvas.instructure.com/doc/api/submissions.html#method.submissions_api.bulk_update) that `canvasapi` 0.9.0 does not yet wrap. I recently had cause to use one of these methods, so proper `canvasapi` versions would be appreciated.

In my particular case I need to update grades for multiple assignments by multiple students. That presumably makes the `POST /api/v1/courses/:course_id/submissions/update_grades` entry point the right thing to use, although it is not clear to me whether one can indeed update multiple assignments for multiple students all in a single call.

issue_title: Refactor: Do not import NumPy functions directly
issue_body:
We currently import several NumPy functions directly, e.g., [here](https://landscape.io/github/EpistasisLab/scikit-rebate/17/modules/skrebate/multisurf.py#L26). Normally this isn't an issue, but `min`, `max`, `mean`, etc. override the standard definitions of these functions in Python.

We should refactor the code to `import numpy as np` then replace all of the NumPy function calls with `np.<function name>`.

issue_title: Cloning catapult takes way too long. Speed this up.
issue_body:
Currently when using the web UI, the first time you click on "View Task Timeline", the catapult repository https://github.com/catapult-project/catapult will be cloned. This takes on the order of **minutes**. This needs to be faster.

Some thoughts and questions:
1. A quick looks suggests that the catapult repo is about 270MB, and 265MB of that is in the .git file, so if we could just clone a snapshot of the repo at the right commit without getting the git history that would help a lot (the remaining 5MB seems to be scattered around large html files).
2. Naively packaging catapult in our wheels would make our wheels enormous and slow to download, however, it should be ok if we first remove the git history.
3. Question, how much of catapult do we actually need?

So, if possible, a great short-term fix would be to figure out how to get only a snapshot of the repo without the git history.

Later, we should just the repo with our wheels after we've removed the git history and ideally other unnecessary files as well. For people building Ray from source, we can clone catapult in `build.sh` and cache it.

@alanamarzoev any thoughts about this?

issue_title: Support num_return_vals argument for actor methods.
issue_body:
Currently, we allow remote functions to return multiple object IDs using the following syntax.

```python
@ray.remote(num_return_vals=3)
def f():
    return 1, 2, 3
```

Then `f.remote()` returns

```
[ObjectID(071b78e52bbe1e2c14e89c823fa3662ba11ee264),
 ObjectID(041b78e52bbe1e2c14e89c823fa3662ba11ee264),
 ObjectID(051b78e52bbe1e2c14e89c823fa3662ba11ee264)]
```

However, there is no equivalent at the moment for actor methods, so actor methods always return a single object ID.

We should allow actor methods to return multiple object IDs. What should the syntax be (since we don't decorate the individual actor methods)? There are a couple options.

1. Add a decorator for actor methods that return multiple object IDs, e.g.,

    ```python
    @ray.remote
    class Foo(object):
        @ray.multiple_return_vals(3)
        def method(self):
            return 1, 2, 3
    ```

2. Make it an argument to the remote function invocation.

    ```python
    foo.method.remote(__ray_num_return_vals__=3)
    ```

issue_title: Warning if semantic versioning not used in document metadata
`shell` and related arguments to `run_command` and `exec_command` are problematic
issue_body:
I don't think the `shell` arguments to `run_command` and `exec_command` make sense -- you cannot know _a priori, in general_ which shell the remote host is running by default, which means (among other things) that you do not know what its escaping and quoting rules are.

As such, there's no guarantee that the `run_command` fragment below -- given a user-supplied shell and command (let alone attempting to use `$SHELL`) -- makes any sense on the remote system:
```
if shell:
    _command += '%s "%s"' % (shell, command,)
else:
    _command += '$SHELL -c "%s"' % (command,)
```

You could assume the remote is always entirely POSIX-shell-compatible, but in reality there's two common classes of system where that won't be true: users of `fish`, and many embedded devices. I don't think this is the right level at which to deal with the shell, because the requirements are very specific to the caller. 

Consider: currently, specifying any of the `shell`, `sudo`, or `user` arguments in either function alters the semantics of the `command` argument, because now the caller has to shell-escape the command string, and the escaping requirements may vary from shell to shell.

issue_title: [V3] Meaningful responses to bad command arguments
issue_body:
Right now, when a "Bad Argument" is passed into a command (i.e. when a `commands.Converter` fails), the bot simply responds with the help message. This doesn't really tell the user why the command failed, and I've seen many users be stumped by this.

I have two ideas for solutions:
1. Respond saying exactly what conversion failed, e.g. if a `commands.MemberConverter` fails, it responds with "Member _<user>_ not found"
2. Make the help message contain better documentation for command arguments.

Disadvantages of each solution:
1. Relies on a meaningful message being passed into `commands.BadArgument` when a conversion fails, which brings up potential issues with custom converters (there are no guidelines on how `commands.BadArgument` should be used).
2. A more meaningful help message is still fairly ambiguous. The point of error messages is for the user to know exactly what went wrong, not just throwing a bunch of help docs in their face.

issue_title: Race Conditions in ble_adapter.EvtSync
issue_body:
There are 2 race conditions as a result of the design of EvtSync. Fixes for them as well as a test proving the second race condition are in the attached file [evt_sync.txt](https://github.com/NordicSemiconductor/pc-ble-driver-py/files/2142738/evt_sync.txt).
 

### Race Condition on Data
First EvtSync.data is a single variable that is protected by multiple Locks and therefore is not protected against multiple simultaneous accesses by calling EvtSync.notify on separate 'evts'.

This is addressed by converting self.data to a dictionary that uses 'evts' as keys.

### Callback race condition
The second issue is systemic throughout the codebase. The paradigm of calling a method that causes a message to be received then waiting for result with EvtSync.wait is a constant race condition. If the message that will be waited on is received before the EvtSync.wait() is called, then message is missed. This was first noticed in [ble_adapter.authenticate](https://github.com/NordicSemiconductor/pc-ble-driver-py/blob/master/python/pc_ble_driver_py/ble_adapter.py#L414-L415), but is a very common paradigm in the code base.

This happens because the callbacks are handled in a separate thread from the initiator, and therefore there is no guarantee for continuous execution to the wait unless the call is also protected with a lock.

The solution for this is a protected_call method in EvtSync that prevents the callback from notifying until after the initiator has become to wait for the message.

### Expected Test Results:
```
Test the "call then wait for event" paradigm used regularly in the nordic pc-ble-driver-py library
Slow func sleep	0.1:	Call then Wait:	Successfully Synched! 0
Slow func sleep	0.25:	Call then Wait:	Successfully Synched! 1
Slow func sleep	1:	Call then Wait:	None

issue_title: json.dumps(chart.options) 报错pyecharts.echarts.label.EmphasisLabel is not JSON serializable
issue_body:
**问题**
输出chart.options是可以输出的。
但是如果要变成JSON或者dict传给前端，就会报错。

**运行环境（系统环境及 pyecharts 版本）**
问题仅仅出现在当前最新版本(0.5.5)，用0.4的版本没问题。


**代码及截图**
```python
import json
from pyecharts import Pie

chart = Pie("Bug")
chart.add('', ['1', '2'], [3, 4], is_label_show=True)
json.dumps(chart.options)

# TypeError: <pyecharts.echarts.label.EmphasisLabel object at 0x000001E54B1399B0> is not JSON serializable

```
看样子是新版本引入了自定义回调函数的问题。
解决办法:
```python
import json
from pyecharts import Pie
from pyecharts_javascripthon.api import TRANSLATOR

chart = Pie("Bug")
chart.add('', ['1', '2'], [3, 4], is_label_show=True)
json.dumps(json.loads(TRANSLATOR.translate(chart.options).as_snippet()))

# 不报错了，也能运行了
```

看样子可以优化一下这个获取options的操作emmmm

issue_title: Process types with 0 running instances cause KeyError
issue_body:
Say I have a pretty standard procfile declaring a "web" and a "worker" process. The default state for my app is to have a single web dyno running, and no workers running, using heroku.py to file up workers on demand. 

However, when 0 workers are running, 

app.processes['worker'].scale(0)

causes KeyError. In other words the process map only contains information about currently running dynos regardless of the contents of the procfile, making it impossible to automate the transition from 0 to 1 workers.

issue_title: slow http
issue_body:
in res_client.py in method _http_request you are using 
httplib2.Http().request(api, method, params, headers)

it is dozens to hundreds times slower than using urllib (urllib2, urllib3) because there's inefficiency in method readline (in socket.py). this function is linear to the length of the message because of working byte by byte:

     392 :     2300 :       0.031000137329 : 0.0000134783 |                 data = None
     393 :     2300 :       0.015000104904 : 0.0000065218 |                 recv = self._sock.recv
     394 :    43183 :       0.311999559402 : 0.0000072251 |                 while data != "\n":
     395 :    40883 :       0.124999761581 : 0.0000030575 |                     data = recv(1)
     396 :    40883 :      34.978999853134 : 0.0008555879 |                     if not data:
     397 :          :                      :              |                         break
     398 :    40883 :       0.263999938965 : 0.0000064575 |                     buffers.append(data)
     399 :     2300 :       0.016000032425 : 0.0000069565 |                 return "".join(buffers)

look above - 34.97 - it is a time in seconds for getting 180 keys from couchbase. 40883 - it is an execution count.
with urllib.urlopen (or similar) this time reduces to about 3 seconds.

issue_title: ZMQStream.on_recv/on_send should pass stream to callbacks
issue_body:
Currently, the on_recv/on_send system only passes the *message* to the callback, but they should really pass both the stream and the message.  This is the way the czmq reactor behaves, and it makes it easier to use the same callback with multiple streams.

The only tricky thing here is how to add this in a sensible way that does not break current apps that only expect the one argument.

For now, this behavior can be emulated with closures and intermediary functions:

```python
def handle_recv(stream, msg):
    dostuff()

for s in streams:
    s.on_recv(lambda msg: handle_recv(s,msg))
```

But this is suboptimal.
